sales_total = sales.groupby("product_id")["units_sold"].sum().reset_index()
merged_clean = products.merge(sales_total, on="product_id", how="left")
print(merged_clean)

   product_id product_name  units_sold
0           1       Coffee         255
1           2          Tea         170
2           3        Juice          60



merged has 5 rows — more than the 3 rows in products. Each product in products has been duplicated once for each matching row in sales.
This is technically correct behaviour for a left join — every matching row in the right table generates a new row in the result. But it is often not what you want, and if you were not expecting it, the inflated row count is a sign that something needs attention.
.value_counts() on the key column quickly shows whether any values appear more than once. In real data with thousands of rows, you can also check sales["product_id"].duplicated().sum() for a quick count of duplicate keys.
Aggregating before merging is cleaner: first collapse sales to one row per product, then merge. This is a general principle — if the right table has multiple rows per key and you want a one-to-one merge, aggregate first.





Exercise 3.8.4
Split the flights dataset by month using a for loop, then reassemble it using pd.concat().
import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")
print(flights["month"].unique())

Use a for loop to split flights into 12 subsets — one for each calendar month (e.g., Jan) — and store them in a list called month_dfs. Print the shape of each DataFrame in the list.
Use pd.concat() to reassemble the list into a single DataFrame called flights_combined. Reset the index with drop=True.
Verify the reassembled DataFrame matches the original by checking that the shapes are identical and that the total passenger count is the same.



Hint

To split by month using a for loop, define a list of calendar months and loop over them. For each month, filter the DataFrame to rows where month is equal to that month and append the result the list. Recall that you can use the append function to add items to a list inside the loop.



Solution


import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")

# 1. Split by month using a for loop
month_dfs = []

for month in flights["month"].unique():
    subset = flights[flights["month"] == month].copy()
    month_dfs.append(subset)
    print(subset.shape)

(12, 3)
(12, 3)
(12, 3)
(12, 3)
(12, 3)
(12, 3)
(12, 3)
(12, 3)
(12, 3)
(12, 3)
(12, 3)
(12, 3)



# 2. Reassemble with pd.concat()
flights_combined = pd.concat(month_dfs).reset_index(drop=True)
print(flights_combined.shape)

(144, 3)



# 3. Verify against original
print(flights.shape == flights_combined.shape)
print(flights["passengers"].sum() == flights_combined["passengers"].sum())

True
True


The key pattern here is storing DataFrames in a list inside a loop and then passing the list to pd.concat(). This is a common pattern in real data work — for example when reading multiple CSV files from a folder, each containing data for a different time period, and assembling them into a single DataFrame. Always reset the index after pd.concat() to avoid duplicate index labels from the original DataFrames.




Exercise 3.8.5
In this exercise you will merge two real datasets hosted on GitHub: one containing core passenger information from titanic and one containing passenger names and ticket details. Both datasets originate from the same source and share passenger_id as the primary key, but they also share two other columns — Pclass and Sex — with different casing.
import pandas as pd

url_core  = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/03-data-wrangling/data/titanic_core.csv"
url_names = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/03-data-wrangling/data/titanic_names.csv"

core  = pd.read_csv(url_core)
names = pd.read_csv(url_names)

print(core.shape)
print(core.head())
print(names.shape)
print(names.head())

Inspect both DataFrames. Which columns appear in both? How does the casing differ, and why does this matter for merging?
Rename PassengerId, Pclass, and Sex in names to passenger_id, pclass, and sex so the column names match core.
Merge the two DataFrames using a left join on passenger_id. Then drop the duplicate pclass and sex columns that result from the merge. Check the row count after merging to verify no rows were lost.
Using the merged DataFrame, compute the survival rate by passenger class for male and female passengers separately. Which group had the highest survival rate?



Hint

After merging on passenger_id alone, pandas will suffix the duplicate pclass and sex columns as pclass_x, pclass_y, sex_x, and sex_y. You can drop the _y versions and rename the _x versions back, or alternatively merge on all three shared keys — passenger_id, pclass, and sex — to avoid the duplicates entirely.



Solution


import pandas as pd

url_core  = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/03-data-wrangling/data/titanic_core.csv"
url_names = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/03-data-wrangling/data/titanic_names.csv"

core  = pd.read_csv(url_core)
names = pd.read_csv(url_names)

# 2. Rename columns in names to match core
names = names.rename(columns={
    "PassengerId": "passenger_id",
    "Pclass":      "pclass",
    "Sex":         "sex"
})


# 3. Merge on all three shared keys to avoid duplicate columns
merged = core.merge(names, on=["passenger_id", "pclass", "sex"], how="left")
print("Core rows:  ", len(core))
print("Merged rows:", len(merged))
print(merged.head())

Core rows:   891
Merged rows: 891
   passenger_id  survived  pclass     sex   age     fare embarked  \
0             1         0       3    male  22.0   7.2500        S   
1             2         1       1  female  38.0  71.2833        C   
2             3         1       3  female  26.0   7.9250        S   
3             4         1       1  female  35.0  53.1000        S   
4             5         0       3    male  35.0   8.0500        S   

                                                Name            Ticket Cabin  
0                            Braund, Mr. Owen Harris         A/5 21171   NaN  
1  Cumings, Mrs. John Bradley (Florence Briggs Th...          PC 17599   C85  
2                             Heikkinen, Miss. Laina  STON/O2. 3101282   NaN  
3       Futrelle, Mrs. Jacques Heath (Lily May Peel)            113803  C123  
4                           Allen, Mr. William Henry            373450   NaN  



# 4. Survival rate by class and sex
summary = merged.groupby(["pclass", "sex"])["survived"].mean().round(3)
print(summary)

pclass  sex   
1       female    0.968
        male      0.369
2       female    0.921
        male      0.157
3       female    0.500
        male      0.135
Name: survived, dtype: float64


Task 1: Both DataFrames contain passenger_id/PassengerId, pclass/Pclass, and sex/Sex. The casing difference matters because pandas treats column names as case-sensitive — pclass and Pclass are different column names and will not be recognised as the same key unless you standardise them first.
Task 3: Merging on all three shared keys is the cleanest approach — it avoids duplicate columns entirely and provides an additional check that the rows match correctly across all three variables. The row count should be 891 in both core and merged, confirming no rows were lost.
Task 4: First class female passengers had by far the highest survival rate at around 97%. In general, female passengers survived at much higher rates than male passengers across all classes, and higher passenger classes survived at higher rates than lower classes — with the exception that second class female passengers also survived at very high rates.

title: Working with dates
text:
Exercises
We cover how to convert string columns to proper datetime objects, how to extract date components such as year or month using the .dt accessor, and how to filter and group data by date.

title: Working with dates
text:
Exercise 3.9.1
The DataFrame below contains sales records with dates stored as strings.
import pandas as pd

sales = pd.DataFrame({
    "date":    ["2020-03-15", "2019-11-02", "2021-07-28", "2018-05-10",
                "2020-01-22", "2019-08-14", "2021-03-05", "2018-12-30"],
    "product": ["Coffee", "Tea", "Juice", "Coffee", "Tea", "Juice",
                "Coffee", "Tea"],
    "revenue": [1200, 850, 640, 1100, 920, 710, 1350, 780]
})

Sort the DataFrame by date and print the result. Does the sort order look correct? What is happening, and why?
Filter the DataFrame to sales from 2020 onwards using sales["date"] >= "2020-01-01". Does the result look correct?
Convert the date column to datetime using pd.to_datetime() and check the dtype before and after. Then repeat the sort and filter from steps 1 and 2. How do the results differ?



Solution


import pandas as pd

sales = pd.DataFrame({
    "date":    ["2020-03-15", "2019-11-02", "2021-07-28", "2018-05-10",
                "2020-01-22", "2019-08-14", "2021-03-05", "2018-12-30"],
    "product": ["Coffee", "Tea", "Juice", "Coffee", "Tea", "Juice",
                "Coffee", "Tea"],
    "revenue": [1200, 850, 640, 1100, 920, 710, 1350, 780]
})

# 1. Sort by date as string
print(sales.sort_values("date"))

         date product  revenue
3  2018-05-10  Coffee     1100
7  2018-12-30     Tea      780
5  2019-08-14   Juice      710
1  2019-11-02     Tea      850
4  2020-01-22     Tea      920
0  2020-03-15  Coffee     1200
6  2021-03-05  Coffee     1350
2  2021-07-28   Juice      640



# 2. Filter as string
print(sales[sales["date"] >= "2020-01-01"])

         date product  revenue
0  2020-03-15  Coffee     1200
2  2021-07-28   Juice      640
4  2020-01-22     Tea      920
6  2021-03-05  Coffee     1350



# 3. Convert and repeat
print("Before:", sales["date"].dtype)
sales["date"] = pd.to_datetime(sales["date"])
print("After: ", sales["date"].dtype)

print(sales.sort_values("date"))
print(sales[sales["date"] >= "2020-01-01"])

Before: str
After:  datetime64[us]
        date product  revenue
3 2018-05-10  Coffee     1100
7 2018-12-30     Tea      780
5 2019-08-14   Juice      710
1 2019-11-02     Tea      850
4 2020-01-22     Tea      920
0 2020-03-15  Coffee     1200
6 2021-03-05  Coffee     1350
2 2021-07-28   Juice      640
        date product  revenue
0 2020-03-15  Coffee     1200
2 2021-07-28   Juice      640
4 2020-01-22     Tea      920
6 2021-03-05  Coffee     1350



The sort order looks correct in this case because the dates are in YYYY-MM-DD format, which happens to sort correctly as a string because the most significant component (year) comes first. This is a lucky coincidence — a format like DD/MM/YYYY would sort completely incorrectly as a string. Never rely on this: always convert to datetime before sorting.
The filter also appears to work here for the same reason. However, string comparisons are character-by-character and have nothing to do with time — they only produce correct results by accident when the format is ISO (YYYY-MM-DD).
After converting to datetime, both the sort and the filter work correctly and for the right reasons. The dtype changes from str to datetime64[ns], confirming that pandas now treats the column as a proper point in time rather than a sequence of characters.





Exercise 3.9.2
The DataFrame below contains patient visit records with dates in DD/MM/YYYY format — common in European data exports.
import pandas as pd

visits = pd.DataFrame({
    "date":       ["02/01/2021", "06/05/2020", "11/03/2019",
                   "04/08/2022", "01/09/2021", "09/07/2020"],
    "patient_id": [101, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106],
    "diagnosis":  ["flu", "checkup", "injury", "flu", "checkup", "injury"]
})

Convert the date column to datetime without specifying a format= argument and print the result. The conversion succeeds without any error — but do the dates look correct? Compare the converted dates against the original strings and explain what pandas has done wrong.
Fix the conversion by specifying the correct format= argument for DD/MM/YYYY dates. Print the result and verify it now matches the original strings.
Add two new columns to visits: year and month_num, extracted from the correctly converted date column using the .dt accessor.



Hint

The format codes you need is "%d/%m/%Y" — %d for day, %m for month number, and %Y for four-digit year. Always verify the converted dates look sensible after conversion — ambiguous formats can silently produce wrong results with no error or warning.



Solution


import pandas as pd

visits = pd.DataFrame({
    "date":       ["02/01/2021", "06/05/2020", "11/03/2019",
                   "04/08/2022", "01/09/2021", "09/07/2020"],
    "patient_id": [101, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106],
    "diagnosis":  ["flu", "checkup", "injury", "flu", "checkup", "injury"]
})

# 1. Convert without format
visits["date_wrong"] = pd.to_datetime(visits["date"])
print(visits[["date", "date_wrong", "patient_id"]])

         date date_wrong  patient_id
0  02/01/2021 2021-02-01         101
1  06/05/2020 2020-06-05         102
2  11/03/2019 2019-11-03         103
3  04/08/2022 2022-04-08         104
4  01/09/2021 2021-01-09         105
5  09/07/2020 2020-09-07         106



# 2. Convert with correct format
visits["date"] = pd.to_datetime(visits["date"], format="%d/%m/%Y")
print(visits[["date", "date_wrong", "patient_id"]])

        date date_wrong  patient_id
0 2021-01-02 2021-02-01         101
1 2020-05-06 2020-06-05         102
2 2019-03-11 2019-11-03         103
3 2022-08-04 2022-04-08         104
4 2021-09-01 2021-01-09         105
5 2020-07-09 2020-09-07         106



# 3. Extract year and month
visits["year"]      = visits["date"].dt.year
visits["month_num"] = visits["date"].dt.month
print(visits.head())

        date  patient_id diagnosis date_wrong  year  month_num
0 2021-01-02         101       flu 2021-02-01  2021          1
1 2020-05-06         102   checkup 2020-06-05  2020          5
2 2019-03-11         103    injury 2019-11-03  2019          3
3 2022-08-04         104       flu 2022-04-08  2022          8
4 2021-09-01         105   checkup 2021-01-09  2021          9



The conversion succeeds but the dates are wrong. pandas assumes the American MM/DD/YYYY format by default and interprets the first number as the month and the second as the day — so "02/01/2021" becomes February 1st instead of January 2nd. Because all day values in this dataset happen to be 12 or below, every date is a valid calendar date in both interpretations, so pandas raises no error. This is one of the most dangerous silent bugs when working with dates: the code runs cleanly and the output looks plausible, but every date is wrong. Always cross-check a few converted values against the original strings to catch this.
With format="%d/%m/%Y", pandas correctly reads the first number as day and the second as month, producing the intended dates.





Exercise 3.9.3
The DataFrame below records daily weather observations with the date split across three separate columns.
import pandas as pd

weather = pd.DataFrame({
    "year":      [2021, 2021, 2021, 2022, 2022, 2022],
    "month":     [3, 6, 12, 1, 7, 11],
    "day":       [15, 22, 1, 8, 30, 14],
    "city":      ["Oslo", "Oslo", "Oslo", "Oslo", "Oslo", "Oslo"],
    "temp_c":    [2.1, 18.4, -3.2, -5.1, 22.8, 4.6],
    "rainfall":  [5.2, 0.0, 8.1, 2.3, 12.4, 6.7]
})

Construct a date column by combining year, month, and day into a single string in YYYY-MM-DD format. Remember that month and day are integers and will need to be converted to strings first.
Convert the date column to datetime using the appropriate format= argument and verify the dtype.
Add a days_since_start column that computes the number of days between each observation and the earliest date in the dataset. Print the result.



Hint

The difference between two datetime values produces a timedelta — an object representing a duration rather than a point in time. You can extract the number of days from a timedelta using .dt.days.



Solution


import pandas as pd

weather = pd.DataFrame({
    "year":      [2021, 2021, 2021, 2022, 2022, 2022],
    "month":     [3, 6, 12, 1, 7, 11],
    "day":       [15, 22, 1, 8, 30, 14],
    "city":      ["Oslo", "Oslo", "Oslo", "Oslo", "Oslo", "Oslo"],
    "temp_c":    [2.1, 18.4, -3.2, -5.1, 22.8, 4.6],
    "rainfall":  [5.2, 0.0, 8.1, 2.3, 12.4, 6.7]
})

# 1. Build date string
weather["date"] = (
    weather["year"].astype(str) + "-" +
    weather["month"].astype(str) + "-" +
    weather["day"].astype(str)
)
print(weather["date"].head())

0    2021-3-15
1    2021-6-22
2    2021-12-1
3     2022-1-8
4    2022-7-30
Name: date, dtype: str



# 2. Convert to datetime
weather["date"] = pd.to_datetime(weather["date"], format="%Y-%m-%d")
print(weather["date"].dtype)

datetime64[us]



# 3. Days since earliest date
weather["days_since_start"] = (
    weather["date"] - weather["date"].min()
).dt.days
print(weather[["date", "temp_c", "days_since_start"]])

        date  temp_c  days_since_start
0 2021-03-15     2.1                 0
1 2021-06-22    18.4                99
2 2021-12-01    -3.2               261
3 2022-01-08    -5.1               299
4 2022-07-30    22.8               502
5 2022-11-14     4.6               609


Subtracting two datetime columns produces a timedelta — a duration. Calling .dt.days extracts the integer number of days from that duration. This pattern is useful whenever you need to compute elapsed time: days since first purchase, days between diagnosis and treatment, days until a deadline, and so on.




Exercise 3.9.4
Use the flights dataset with its date column constructed in the video to practice extracting date components and grouping by them.
import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")
flights["date"] = pd.to_datetime(
    flights["year"].astype(str) + "-" + flights["month"],
    format="%Y-%b"
)

Use the .dt accessor to add a quarter column to flights. Print the first 15 rows to verify it looks correct.
Compute the total passengers per quarter, summed across all years. Which quarter consistently has the most air traffic?
Compute the total passengers per year and quarter combination — that is, one row per year-quarter pair. Sort by year and quarter and print the first 12 rows. Use reset_index() to convert the result back to a regular DataFrame and rename the columns to year, quarter, and total_passengers.



Solution


import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")
flights["date"] = pd.to_datetime(
    flights["year"].astype(str) + "-" + flights["month"],
    format="%Y-%b"
)

# 1. Add quarter column
flights["quarter"] = flights["date"].dt.quarter
print(flights.head(15))

    year month  passengers       date  quarter
0   1949   Jan         112 1949-01-01        1
1   1949   Feb         118 1949-02-01        1
2   1949   Mar         132 1949-03-01        1
3   1949   Apr         129 1949-04-01        2
4   1949   May         121 1949-05-01        2
5   1949   Jun         135 1949-06-01        2
6   1949   Jul         148 1949-07-01        3
7   1949   Aug         148 1949-08-01        3
8   1949   Sep         136 1949-09-01        3
9   1949   Oct         119 1949-10-01        4
10  1949   Nov         104 1949-11-01        4
11  1949   Dec         118 1949-12-01        4
12  1950   Jan         115 1950-01-01        1
13  1950   Feb         126 1950-02-01        1
14  1950   Mar         141 1950-03-01        1



# 2. Total passengers per quarter across all years
quarterly = flights.groupby("quarter")["passengers"].sum()
print(quarterly)

quarter
1     8963
2    10207
3    12058
4     9135
Name: passengers, dtype: int64



# 3. Total passengers per year-quarter combination
annual_quarterly = (
    flights.groupby([flights["date"].dt.year, "quarter"])["passengers"]
    .sum()
    .reset_index()
    .rename(columns={"date": "year", "passengers": "total_passengers"})
    .sort_values(["year", "quarter"])
)
print(annual_quarterly.head(12))

    year  quarter  total_passengers
0   1949        1               362
1   1949        2               385
2   1949        3               432
3   1949        4               341
4   1950        1               382
5   1950        2               409
6   1950        3               498
7   1950        4               387
8   1951        1               473
9   1951        2               513
10  1951        3               582
11  1951        4               474



Quarter 3 (July–September) consistently has the most passengers — reflecting the summer holiday travel season. Quarter 1 (January–March) has the fewest, consistent with the post-holiday dip in travel demand.
Grouping by flights["date"].dt.year directly rather than by the year column gives the same result here, but using the datetime accessor is the more general approach — it works even when no separate year column exists in the data.





Exercise 3.9.5
Use the flights dataset with its date column to practice filtering by date range and comparing periods.
import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")
flights["date"] = pd.to_datetime(
    flights["year"].astype(str) + "-" + flights["month"],
    format="%Y-%b"
)

Filter flights to the period from January 1949 to December 1951 inclusive. Assign the result to early and print its shape.
Filter flights to the period from January 1958 to December 1960 inclusive. Assign the result to late and print its shape.
Compute the mean monthly passengers for each period and the percentage increase from the early to the late period. What does this tell you about the growth of air travel over this decade?
Within the late period, filter further to summer months only (July, August, September) and compute the mean monthly passengers. How does this compare to the overall mean for the late period?



Solution


import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")
flights["date"] = pd.to_datetime(
    flights["year"].astype(str) + "-" + flights["month"],
    format="%Y-%b"
)

# 1. Early period
early = flights[
    (flights["date"] >= "1949-01-01") &
    (flights["date"] <= "1951-12-31")
]
print(early.shape)

(36, 4)



# 2. Late period
late = flights[
    (flights["date"] >= "1958-01-01") &
    (flights["date"] <= "1960-12-31")
]
print(late.shape)

(36, 4)



# 3. Mean monthly passengers and percentage increase
early_mean = early["passengers"].mean()
late_mean  = late["passengers"].mean()
pct_change = (late_mean - early_mean) / early_mean * 100

print(f"Early mean: {early_mean:.1f}")
print(f"Late mean:  {late_mean:.1f}")
print(f"Increase:   {pct_change:.1f}%")

Early mean: 145.5
Late mean:  428.5
Increase:   194.5%



# 4. Summer months in the late period
summer_late = late[late["date"].dt.month.isin([7, 8, 9])]
print(f"Summer mean: {summer_late['passengers'].mean():.1f}")
print(f"Overall mean: {late['passengers'].mean():.1f}")

Summer mean: 522.9
Overall mean: 428.5



Mean monthly passengers more than doubled from the early to the late period — reflecting the rapid expansion of commercial aviation in the 1950s driven by the introduction of jet aircraft and rising incomes.
Summer passengers in the late period are considerably higher than the overall late-period mean, confirming that the seasonal peak in Q3 identified in exercise 3.9.4 holds throughout the decade and becomes more pronounced as overall travel volumes grow.

title: Topic 3 capstone
text:
We bring together everything covered in Topic 3 in a single end-to-end data wrangling workflow — from loading and cleaning raw data to producing a final summary table ready for export.

# Research question: Which countries get the most life expectancy
# per dollar of health spending?

import pandas as pd

# ── Step 1: Load and inspect ─────────────────────────────────

healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv")

healthexp.shape
healthexp.head()
healthexp.info()
healthexp.isna().sum()

# ── Step 2: Standardise column names ─────────────────────────

healthexp.columns = [col.lower().replace(" ", "_") for col in healthexp.columns]
healthexp.columns

# ── Step 3: Fix country names ────────────────────────────────

healthexp["country"] = healthexp["country"].replace({
    "Great Britain": "United Kingdom",
    "USA":           "United States",
})

healthexp["country"].unique()

# ── Step 4: Merge with continent data ────────────────────────

url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/owid/owid-datasets/refs/heads/master/datasets/Countries%20Continents/Countries%20Continents.csv"

continents = pd.read_csv(url)
continents = continents.drop(columns=["Year"])
continents = continents.rename(columns={"Entity": "country", "Countries Continents": "continent"})
continents.head()

healthexp = healthexp.merge(continents, on="country", how="left")
healthexp.head()
healthexp.shape
healthexp.isna().sum()

# ── Step 5: Create new variables ─────────────────────────────

healthexp["life_per_1000usd"] = (healthexp["life_expectancy"] / healthexp["spending_usd"] * 1000).round(2)
healthexp.head()

# ── Step 6: Summarise by country ─────────────────────────────

summary = (
    healthexp
    .groupby(["continent", "country"])
    .agg(
        mean_spending   = ("spending_usd",     "mean"),
        mean_life_exp   = ("life_expectancy",  "mean"),
        mean_efficiency = ("life_per_1000usd", "mean"),
    )
    .round(1)
    .sort_values("mean_efficiency", ascending=False)
    .reset_index()
)

summary

# ── Step 7: Export ────────────────────────────────────────────

healthexp.to_csv("data/healthexp_clean.csv",    index=False)
summary.to_excel("data/healthexp_summary.xlsx", index=False)

print("Exported healthexp_clean.csv and healthexp_summary.csv to data/")

title: Topic 3 — Further Exercises
text:
🟢 Warm-up
These exercises give you an opportunity to consolidate everything covered in Topic 3 before moving on. Many of these combine skills from several videos into a single task, and some may introduce new tools that were not shown in the videos — for these, you are expected to look up the documentation yourself, the same way you would when working independently.
The exercises are organised into three levels:

title: Topic 3 — Further Exercises
text:
Exercise 3.11.1
Use the titanic dataset to answer the following questions using groupby and aggregation. Each question requires a single groupby operation.
import pandas as pd

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")

Compute the mean and median fare paid by passengers in each embarkation port (embarked). Which port has the highest mean fare? Is the mean or median more representative here, and why?
For each combination of passenger class and sex, compute the survival rate and the number of passengers. Sort the result by survival rate in descending order.
Compute the mean age and mean fare for each passenger class.



Solution


import pandas as pd

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")

# 1. Mean and median fare by embarkation port
print(
    titanic.groupby("embarked")["fare"]
    .agg(mean_fare="mean", median_fare="median")
    .round(2)
)

          mean_fare  median_fare
embarked                        
C             59.95        29.70
Q             13.28         7.75
S             27.08        13.00



# 2. Survival rate and count by class and sex
print(
    titanic.groupby(["pclass", "sex"])
    .agg(survival_rate=("survived", "mean"), n=("survived", "count"))
    .round(3)
    .sort_values("survival_rate", ascending=False)
)

               survival_rate    n
pclass sex                       
1      female          0.968   94
2      female          0.921   76
3      female          0.500  144
1      male            0.369  122
2      male            0.157  108
3      male            0.135  347



# 3. Mean age and fare by passenger class
print(titanic.groupby("pclass")[["fare", "age"]].mean().round(1))

        fare   age
pclass            
1       84.2  38.2
2       20.7  29.9
3       13.7  25.1



Port C (Cherbourg) has the highest mean fare by a considerable margin. The mean is likely less representative than the median here because fare distributions tend to be right-skewed — a small number of first-class passengers paying very high fares pulls the mean upward.
First-class female passengers have the highest survival rate. The pattern is consistent: within each class, female passengers survived at much higher rates than male passengers, and within each sex, higher classes survived at higher rates.
Both the average age and fare are increasing with class. Note that because the same aggregation (mean) is computed on the two columns, it is not necessary to use agg().





Exercise 3.11.2
Use the healthexp dataset to practice filtering and sorting. Each task below requires a single filter or sort operation.
import pandas as pd

healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv")

Filter to observations where life expectancy is above 80 and health spending is below 5 000 USD. Which country-year combinations meet both criteria?
Sort the dataset by life expectancy in descending order and print the top 10 rows. Which country and year holds the record for highest life expectancy in the dataset?
Filter to the most recent year available and sort by spending in ascending order. Which country spends the least on health in the most recent year?



Solution


import pandas as pd

healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv")

# 1. Life expectancy above 80 and spending below 5000
efficient = healthexp[
    (healthexp["Life_Expectancy"] > 80) &

]
print(efficient[["Country", "Year", "Life_Expectancy", "Spending_USD"]])

Exercise 3.11.3
Use the flights dataset to practice working with dates. Start by building the date column as shown in the video.
import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")
flights["date"] = pd.to_datetime(
    flights["year"].astype(str) + "-" + flights["month"],
    format="%Y-%b"
)

Extract the month number from the date column as a new column month_num. Then compute the mean number of passengers for each month number across all years. Which month number has the highest average passenger count?
Filter the dataset to all observations from the second half of the year (July through December) and compute the total passengers in this period. Compare this to the total for the first half of the year.
Use .dt.day_name() to add a column day_name showing the name of the day of the week each date falls on. Which day of the week do the most flight dates in the dataset fall on, and why does this make sense given the resolution of the data?



Solution


import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")
flights["date"] = pd.to_datetime(
    flights["year"].astype(str) + "-" + flights["month"],
    format="%Y-%b"
)

# 1. Mean passengers by month number
flights["month_num"] = flights["date"].dt.month
print(flights.groupby("month_num")["passengers"].mean().round(1))

month_num
1     241.8
2     235.0
3     270.2
4     267.1
5     271.8
6     311.7
7     351.3
8     351.1
9     302.4
10    266.6
11    232.8
12    261.8
Name: passengers, dtype: float64



# 2. First vs second half of the year
first_half  = flights[flights["month_num"] <= 6]["passengers"].sum()
second_half = flights[flights["month_num"] > 6]["passengers"].sum()
print(f"First half:  {first_half:,}")
print(f"Second half: {second_half:,}")

First half:  19,170
Second half: 21,193



# 3. Day of the week
flights["day_name"] = flights["date"].dt.day_name()
print(flights["day_name"].value_counts())

day_name
Tuesday      23
Friday       21
Sunday       21
Thursday     21
Saturday     20
Wednesday    19
Monday       19
Name: count, dtype: int64



Month 7 (July) has the highest average passenger count, consistent with summer being the peak travel season.
The second half of the year has more total passengers than the first half, driven by the summer peak in July through September.
All dates fall on the first day of the month. Because pd.to_datetime() with a %Y-%b format string sets the day to 1 by default, every date in the dataset is the first of a month. The day of the week on which the first of the month falls depends on the year and month, so the distribution across weekdays is roughly even — but all values represent the same type of date (first of month), not randomly distributed days.

title: Topic 3 — Further Exercises
text:
🟡 Practice

Exercise 3.11.4
This exercise combines filtering, missing value handling, column creation, and groupby to produce a summary of Titanic survival patterns.
import pandas as pd

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")

Drop rows where embarked is missing. Then fill missing values in age with the median age within each passenger class using groupby and transform. Verify that no missing values remain in either column.
Create a column age_group with the following categories:

"child" if age is below 18
"adult" if age is between 18 and 59
"senior" if age is 60 or above

Compute the survival rate for each combination of age_group and pclass. Sort by survival rate in descending order. Which group had the highest survival rate?
Compute the share of each age_group within each passenger class. Does the age distribution differ meaningfully across classes?



Hint

For step 2, you could write a function with an if/elif/else chain and use .apply(). But pandas also has a vectorised function called np.select() that handles exactly this kind of multi-branch classification without needing a custom function. It takes a list of conditions and a matching list of values:
conditions = [condition1, condition2]
choices    = [value1, value2]
np.select(conditions, choices, default=default_value)
You can look up the documentation for np.select() by running help(np.select) in the console or searching online.



Solution


import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")

# 1. Drop missing embarked, fill missing age by class
titanic = titanic.dropna(subset=["embarked"])
titanic["age"] = titanic["age"].fillna(
    titanic.groupby("pclass")["age"].transform("median")
)
print(titanic[["age", "embarked"]].isna().sum())

age         0
embarked    0
dtype: int64



# 2. Create age_group
conditions = [titanic["age"] < 18, titanic["age"] < 60]
choices    = ["child", "adult"]
titanic["age_group"] = np.select(conditions, choices, default="senior")
print(titanic["age_group"].value_counts())

age_group
adult     751
child     113
senior     25
Name: count, dtype: int64



# 3. Survival rate by age group and class
print(
    titanic.groupby(["age_group", "pclass"])["survived"]
    .mean()
    .round(3)
    .sort_values(ascending=False)
)

age_group  pclass
child      1         0.917
           2         0.913
adult      1         0.640
           2         0.414
child      3         0.372
senior     2         0.250
           1         0.250
adult      3         0.218
senior     3         0.200
Name: survived, dtype: float64



# 4. Share of each age group within each class
age_class = titanic.groupby(["pclass", "age_group"]).size()
age_class_share = (age_class / titanic.groupby("pclass")["age_group"].count()).round(3)
print(age_class_share)

pclass  age_group
1       adult        0.869
        child        0.056
        senior       0.075
2       adult        0.853
        child        0.125
        senior       0.022
3       adult        0.831
        child        0.159
        senior       0.010
dtype: float64



First-class children had the highest survival rate. In general, children and first-class passengers survived at higher rates. The “women and children first” evacuation policy is clearly visible in the data.
Third-class passengers tend to be younger on average than first-class passengers, with a higher share of children and young adults — consistent with the historical pattern of younger emigrants travelling in lower classes. Note the difference between .size() and .count() used above. .size() is applied directly to the grouped object and counts the number of rows in each group, including rows with missing values. .count() is applied to a specific column and counts only the non-missing values in that column. This also means .size() can be used to count rows even when you have not selected a column. Since age_group has no missing values here, the two approaches give the same result — but with a column that does have missing values, .count() would return a smaller number than .size().





Exercise 3.11.5
This exercise asks you to implement the percentage change calculation two ways: first with a manual loop, and then with a pandas built-in function. The goal is to make a habit of checking whether pandas already has a function for what you want to do, before writing your own.
import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")
flights["date"] = pd.to_datetime(
    flights["year"].astype(str) + "-" + flights["month"],
    format="%Y-%b"
)
flights = flights.sort_values("date").reset_index(drop=True)
Part 1. Without using any pandas function designed for this purpose, compute the month-on-month percentage change in passengers using a for loop. For each row (starting from the second row), use .iloc[] to access the current and previous row’s passenger count, compute the percentage change using the formula below, and store the result in a new column pct_change_manual. The first row should be NaN since there is no previous value to compare against.
\[\text{percentage change} = \frac{\text{current} - \text{previous}}{\text{previous}} \times 100\]
Part 2. pandas has a built-in method that computes percentage change directly on a Series in a single line. Look up the documentation for pd.Series.pct_change() and use it to create a second column pct_change_pandas. Multiply by 100 to match the scale of Part 1.
Part 3. Drop rows where the percentage change is missing, and verify that the two columns are identical. Then filter to rows where the month-on-month change exceeds 20% and print those rows. In which years and months does passenger growth spike most strongly?


Hint

For Part 1, initialize the new column with NaN for every row, for example using flights["pct_change_manual"] = None. Then loop over the row positions with for i in range(1, len(flights)), and use .iloc[] to access the current row (flights.iloc[i]) and the previous row (flights.iloc[i - 1]). Assign the computed value back into the column at position i using flights.loc[i, "pct_change_manual"] = ....



Solution


import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")
flights["date"] = pd.to_datetime(
    flights["year"].astype(str) + "-" + flights["month"],
    format="%Y-%b"
)
flights = flights.sort_values("date").reset_index(drop=True)

# Part 1: manual loop
flights["pct_change_manual"] = None

for i in range(1, len(flights)):
    current  = flights.iloc[i]["passengers"]
    previous = flights.iloc[i - 1]["passengers"]
    flights.loc[i, "pct_change_manual"] = (current - previous) / previous * 100

flights["pct_change_manual"] = flights["pct_change_manual"].astype(float)

# Part 2: pandas built-in
flights["pct_change_pandas"] = flights["passengers"].pct_change() * 100

flights[["date", "passengers", "pct_change_manual", "pct_change_pandas"]].head(10)

passengers
pct_change_manual
pct_change_pandas

NaN

1949-02-01

11.864407

# Part 3: verify and filter to spikes above 20%
flights = flights.dropna()
print((flights["pct_change_manual"].round(6) == flights["pct_change_pandas"].round(6)).all())

spikes = flights[flights["pct_change_pandas"] > 20]
print(spikes[["date", "passengers", "pct_change_pandas"]])

True
         date  passengers  pct_change_pandas
23 1950-12-01         140          22.807018
50 1953-03-01         236          20.408163
62 1954-03-01         235          25.000000


The two columns are identical. A short note on the loop mechanics: initializing the column with flights["pct_change_manual"] = None creates an object-dtype column, since None is not numeric. Assigning floats into individual cells with .loc[] does not automatically upgrade the column’s dtype, so .astype(float) is needed afterward to convert it to a proper numeric column — this also turns the remaining None in the first row into a genuine NaN. Even after this conversion, comparing the two columns with == will mark the first row as unequal, because NaN == NaN always evaluates to False in pandas, regardless of how the missing value was created. Alternatively you can use .equals() rather than == as this treats matching NaN positions as equals:
flights["pct_change_manual"].round(6).equals(flights["pct_change_pandas"].round(6))
The broader lesson: a for loop with .iloc[] works, but it is slow, verbose, and easy to get wrong — for example by forgetting to handle the first row, or making an off-by-one error in the row indices. Before reaching for a loop, it is always worth checking whether pandas already has a built-in vectorised function for the operation. .pct_change(), .cumsum(), .diff(), and .shift() cover many common row-wise operations without needing a loop or a custom function at all.




Exercise 3.11.6
This exercise combines merging, groupby, and column creation to extend the analysis from the capstone video.
Load the cleaned healthexp data and the continents table, merge them, and answer the questions below.
import pandas as pd

healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv")
healthexp.columns = [col.lower().replace(" ", "_") for col in healthexp.columns]
healthexp["country"] = healthexp["country"].replace({
    "Great Britain": "United Kingdom",
    "USA":           "United States",
})

url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/owid/owid-datasets/refs/heads/master/datasets/Countries%20Continents/Countries%20Continents.csv"
continents = pd.read_csv(url)
continents = continents.drop(columns=["Year"])
continents = continents.rename(columns={
    "Entity":               "country",
    "Countries Continents": "continent"
})

healthexp = healthexp.merge(continents, on="country", how="left").sort_values(["year", "country"])

Compute the mean life expectancy and mean spending per continent across all years. Sort by mean life expectancy in descending order.
For each country, compute the total growth in life expectancy from the earliest to the most recent year available. Which country improved the most?
Create a column spending_vs_continent_mean that shows how much each country-year observation’s spending deviates from the mean spending of its continent in that year. Use groupby and transform. Which country-year observations have the largest positive deviation?



Hint

To compute the growth from the earliest to the most recent year for each country, you need the first and last observed value within each group. Two useful groupby methods for this are .first() and .last(), which return the first and last row’s value in each group respectively — for example healthexp.groupby("country")["life_expectancy"].first() gives each country’s earliest recorded life expectancy. This assumes the data is sorted by year within each country, which it already is here.



Solution


import pandas as pd

healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv")
healthexp.columns = [col.lower().replace(" ", "_") for col in healthexp.columns]
healthexp["country"] = healthexp["country"].replace({
    "Great Britain": "United Kingdom",
    "USA":           "United States",
})

url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/owid/owid-datasets/refs/heads/master/datasets/Countries%20Continents/Countries%20Continents.csv"
continents = pd.read_csv(url)
continents = continents.drop(columns=["Year"])
continents = continents.rename(columns={
    "Entity":               "country",
    "Countries Continents": "continent"
})
healthexp = healthexp.merge(continents, on="country", how="left").sort_values(["year", "country"])

# 1. Mean life expectancy and spending by continent
print(
    healthexp.groupby("continent")
    .agg(mean_life_exp=("life_expectancy", "mean"),
         mean_spending=("spending_usd", "mean"))
    .round(1)
    .sort_values("mean_life_exp", ascending=False)
)

               mean_life_exp  mean_spending
continent                                  
Asia                    79.6         1860.3
Europe                  77.8         2557.9
North America           77.2         3599.9



# 2. Life expectancy growth per country
earliest = healthexp.groupby("country")["life_expectancy"].first()
latest   = healthexp.groupby("country")["life_expectancy"].last()
growth   = (latest - earliest).sort_values(ascending=False)
print(growth.round(2))

country
Japan             12.7
Germany           10.5
France            10.1
Canada             8.9
United Kingdom     8.5
United States      6.1
Name: life_expectancy, dtype: float64



# 3. Deviation from continent mean spending by year
healthexp["continent_mean_spending"] = (
    healthexp.groupby(["continent", "year"])["spending_usd"]
    .transform("mean")
)
healthexp["spending_vs_continent_mean"] = (
    healthexp["spending_usd"] - healthexp["continent_mean_spending"]
).round(2)

print(
    healthexp.sort_values("spending_vs_continent_mean", ascending=False)
    [["country", "year", "spending_usd", "spending_vs_continent_mean"]]
    .head(10)
)

           country  year  spending_usd  spending_vs_continent_mean
273  United States  2020     11859.179                     3015.43
267  United States  2019     10855.517                     2832.90
261  United States  2018     10451.386                     2571.52
255  United States  2017     10046.472                     2448.00
243  United States  2015      9355.118                     2359.92
249  United States  2016      9717.649                     2336.69
237  United States  2014      8925.879                     2194.53
231  United States  2013      8519.620                     2045.43
225  United States  2012      8346.064                     2004.91
219  United States  2011      8079.467                     1925.25


Japan shows among the strongest improvements in life expectancy over the period covered. The USA consistently shows the largest positive deviation from its continent’s mean spending, reflecting its outlier status in health expenditure among comparable countries.




Exercise 3.11.7
This exercise combines np.where() and np.select() with groupby to classify and compare countries in the healthexp dataset.
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv")
healthexp.columns = [col.lower().replace(" ", "_") for col in healthexp.columns]

Use np.where() to create a column high_life_exp that is 1 if life expectancy is above the overall median and 0 otherwise.
Use np.select() to create a column spending_tier with three categories:

"low" if spending is below the 33rd percentile
"medium" if spending is between the 33rd and 66th percentile
"high" if spending is above the 66th percentile

Compute the mean life expectancy for each spending_tier. Does higher spending reliably predict higher life expectancy across all tiers?
Compute the share of observations in the high_life_exp group for each country. Which countries are above the median life expectancy for the largest share of their observed years?



Hint

To compute the 33rd and 66th percentiles of the spending column, use healthexp["spending_usd"].quantile(0.33) and healthexp["spending_usd"].quantile(0.66).



Solution


import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv")
healthexp.columns = [col.lower().replace(" ", "_") for col in healthexp.columns]

# 1. High life expectancy indicator
median_life = healthexp["life_expectancy"].median()
healthexp["high_life_exp"] = np.where(
    healthexp["life_expectancy"] > median_life, 1, 0
)
print(healthexp["high_life_exp"].value_counts())

high_life_exp
0    138
1    136
Name: count, dtype: int64



# 2. Spending tier
p33 = healthexp["spending_usd"].quantile(0.33)
p66 = healthexp["spending_usd"].quantile(0.66)

conditions = [
    healthexp["spending_usd"] < p33,
    healthexp["spending_usd"] < p66
]
choices = ["low", "medium"]
healthexp["spending_tier"] = np.select(conditions, choices, default="high")
print(healthexp["spending_tier"].value_counts())

spending_tier
high      93
low       91
medium    90
Name: count, dtype: int64



# 3. Mean life expectancy by spending tier
print(
    healthexp.groupby("spending_tier")["life_expectancy"]
    .mean()
    .round(2)
)

spending_tier
high      80.55
low       74.73
medium    78.39
Name: life_expectancy, dtype: float64



# 4. Share of years above median life expectancy per country
print(
    healthexp.groupby("country")["high_life_exp"]
    .mean()
    .sort_values(ascending=False)
    .round(3)
)

country
France           0.714
Japan            0.667
Canada           0.568
Great Britain    0.465
Germany          0.420
USA              0.216
Name: high_life_exp, dtype: float64



Higher spending tiers are associated with higher mean life expectancy, but the relationship is not perfectly linear. Japan achieves very high life expectancy with moderate spending — it frequently appears in the high life expectancy group despite not being in the highest spending tier.
Japan and France are above the global median life expectancy for all or nearly all of their observed years, while countries with lower spending histories appear above the median for a smaller share of years.





Exercise 3.11.8
This exercise explores two different ways of merging a monthly and a quarterly time series, and the different results they produce. We will use two files:

FRED_monthly_2010.csv — monthly US economic indicators for the 2010s: CPI (the Consumer Price Index, a measure of the general price level), UNRATE (the unemployment rate, in percent), FEDFUNDS (the federal funds rate, the Federal Reserve’s key short-term interest rate), REALRATE (the federal funds rate adjusted for inflation), and LFPART (the labor force participation rate, the share of the working-age population that is employed or looking for work).
GDP.csv — quarterly US Gross Domestic Product, in billions of dollars.

import pandas as pd

url_fred = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/data/FRED/FRED_monthly_2010.csv"
url_gdp  = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/data/FRED/GDP.csv"

fred = pd.read_csv(url_fred)
fred["DATE"] = pd.to_datetime(fred["DATE"])

gdp = pd.read_csv(url_gdp)
gdp["DATE"] = pd.to_datetime(gdp["DATE"])

print(fred.head())
print(gdp.head())
Notice that every row in gdp falls on the first day of a quarter (01-01, 04-01, 07-01, 10-01), while fred has a row for every month. This means there are two reasonable ways to merge the two datasets, and they produce different results.
Approach A: merge on DATE directly.

Merge fred with the DATE and GDP columns from gdp, using an inner join on DATE. Assign the result to merged_a and print its shape. How many rows does it have, and which months survived the merge? Why?

Approach B: merge on year and quarter.

Add year and quarter columns to both fred and gdp using the .dt accessor. Merge fred with the year, quarter, and GDP columns from gdp, using a left join on ["year", "quarter"]. Assign the result to merged_b and print its shape. How does it compare to merged_a?

Comparing the two approaches.

Print the first 6 rows of both merged_a and merged_b. In your own words, explain the difference between the two results: what does each row represent in each case?
Suppose you want to track the unemployment rate month by month throughout the 2010s while always having the corresponding quarter’s GDP available alongside it. Which of the two merged DataFrames is the right one to use for this, and why?
Using the DataFrame you identified in step 4, compare the unemployment rate and GDP at the start of the decade to the end. By how many percentage points did unemployment fall? By what percentage did GDP grow?



Solution


import pandas as pd

url_fred = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/data/FRED/FRED_monthly_2010.csv"
url_gdp  = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/data/FRED/GDP.csv"

fred = pd.read_csv(url_fred)
fred["DATE"] = pd.to_datetime(fred["DATE"])

gdp = pd.read_csv(url_gdp)
gdp["DATE"] = pd.to_datetime(gdp["DATE"])

# 1. Approach A: merge on DATE directly (inner join)
merged_a = fred.merge(gdp[["DATE", "GDP"]], on="DATE", how="inner")
print(merged_a.shape)
print(merged_a[["DATE", "GDP"]])
