32 2018-01-01  20044.1
33 2018-04-01  20150.5
34 2018-07-01  20276.2
35 2018-10-01  20304.9
36 2019-01-01  20431.6
37 2019-04-01  20602.3
38 2019-07-01  20843.3
39 2019-10-01  20985.4



# 2. Approach B: merge on year and quarter (left join)
fred["year"]    = fred["DATE"].dt.year
fred["quarter"] = fred["DATE"].dt.quarter
gdp["year"]     = gdp["DATE"].dt.year
gdp["quarter"]  = gdp["DATE"].dt.quarter

merged_b = fred.merge(gdp[["year", "quarter", "GDP"]], on=["year", "quarter"], how="left")
print(merged_b.shape)
print(merged_b[["DATE", "GDP"]].head(8))

(120, 9)
        DATE      GDP
0 2010-01-01  16582.7
1 2010-02-01  16582.7
2 2010-03-01  16582.7
3 2010-04-01  16743.2
4 2010-05-01  16743.2
5 2010-06-01  16743.2
6 2010-07-01  16872.3
7 2010-08-01  16872.3



# 3. Compare the two
print(merged_a[["DATE", "GDP", "UNRATE"]].head(6))
print(merged_b[["DATE", "GDP", "UNRATE"]].head(6))

        DATE      GDP  UNRATE
0 2010-01-01  16582.7     9.8
1 2010-04-01  16743.2     9.9
2 2010-07-01  16872.3     9.4
3 2010-10-01  16960.9     9.4
4 2011-01-01  16920.6     9.1
5 2011-04-01  17035.1     9.1
        DATE      GDP  UNRATE
0 2010-01-01  16582.7     9.8
1 2010-02-01  16582.7     9.8
2 2010-03-01  16582.7     9.9
3 2010-04-01  16743.2     9.9
4 2010-05-01  16743.2     9.6
5 2010-06-01  16743.2     9.4



# 5. Compare UNRATE and GDP
start = merged_b.iloc[0]
end   = merged_b.iloc[-1]

unemployment_change = end["UNRATE"] - start["UNRATE"]
gdp_growth = (end["GDP"] - start["GDP"]) / start["GDP"] * 100

print(f"Unemployment change: {unemployment_change:.1f} percentage points")
print(f"GDP growth: {gdp_growth:.1f}%")

Unemployment change: -6.2 percentage points
GDP growth: 26.5%



merged_a has only 40 rows — one for every quarter in the decade, since an inner join on DATE only keeps rows where the exact date matches in both DataFrames. Only January, April, July, and October survive; the other eight months of each year are dropped entirely because gdp has no row dated, for example, 2010-02-01.
merged_b has 120 rows — one for every month in the decade, since the left join keeps every row from fred regardless of whether it falls on a quarter boundary. Months that are not the start of a quarter (February, March, May, June, and so on) now have the same GDP value as the first month of their quarter, since GDP is constant within a quarter and is simply broadcast across all three months.
In merged_a, each row represents one full quarter, identified by its first month. In merged_b, each row represents one month, with the quarter’s GDP value repeated for every month that falls within it. Neither is “more correct” — they represent the same underlying data at two different levels of granularity.
merged_b is the right choice. Since the goal is to track unemployment month by month, you need a row for every month — which only merged_b provides. merged_a would silently drop two out of every three months, making it impossible to see the monthly pattern in unemployment at all.
Unemployment fell from 9.8% in January 2010 to 3.6% in December 2019 — a decline of 6.2 percentage points, reflecting the long economic recovery following the 2008 financial crisis. Over the same period, GDP grew by roughly 27%, consistent with a full decade of sustained, if at times slow, economic expansion.

title: Topic 3 — Further Exercises
text:
🔴 Challenge

Exercise 3.11.9
This exercise introduces resample() — a method for aggregating time series data to a coarser time resolution. It is the time-series equivalent of groupby().
To use resample(), the DataFrame must have a datetime column set as the index.
import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")
flights["date"] = pd.to_datetime(
    flights["year"].astype(str) + "-" + flights["month"],
    format="%Y-%b"
)
flights = flights.set_index("date").sort_index()

Look up the documentation for pd.DataFrame.resample() by running help(pd.DataFrame.resample) or searching online. What does the rule= parameter control? What are some common rule strings for monthly, quarterly, and annual resampling?
Use resample() to compute the total passengers per year. Compare the result to the annual totals we computed using groupby() in video 3.9 — are they identical?
Use resample() to compute the total passengers per quarter. Which quarter across all years had the single highest passenger total?
Compute the year-on-year percentage change in annual passenger totals using the result from step 2 and pct_change(). In which year was the growth rate highest?
resample() can also be used to go the other way — upsampling from a coarser to a finer resolution. Use resample("D") on the monthly passengers Series to create a daily-resolution version of the data. Use .asfreq() instead of .sum() — this keeps the existing values at their original dates without aggregating, and introduces NaN for the dates that did not exist in the original data. How many missing values are introduced?
Assume that passengers are distributed evenly across the days within each month, so that each day’s estimated value is the monthly total divided by the number of days in that month. To compute this: first use .ffill() to propagate each month’s total to every day in that month, then divide by the number of days in each month — you can get this using .dt.days_in_month on the index — to obtain the estimated daily passenger count.



Hint

Common rule strings for resample(): "YE" for year-end, "QE" for quarter-end, "ME" for month-end. After resampling, call .sum() or .mean() just as you would after groupby(). Use reset_index() to convert the result back to a regular DataFrame.



Solution


import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")
flights["date"] = pd.to_datetime(
    flights["year"].astype(str) + "-" + flights["month"],
    format="%Y-%b"
)
flights = flights.set_index("date").sort_index()

# 2. Annual totals with resample
annual = flights["passengers"].resample("YE").sum().reset_index()
annual.columns = ["date", "total_passengers"]
print(annual)

         date  total_passengers
0  1949-12-31              1520
1  1950-12-31              1676
2  1951-12-31              2042
3  1952-12-31              2364
4  1953-12-31              2700
5  1954-12-31              2867
6  1955-12-31              3408
7  1956-12-31              3939
8  1957-12-31              4421
9  1958-12-31              4572
10 1959-12-31              5140
11 1960-12-31              5714



# 3. Quarterly totals
quarterly = flights["passengers"].resample("QE").sum().reset_index()
quarterly.columns = ["date", "total_passengers"]
print(quarterly.sort_values("total_passengers", ascending=False).head(5))

         date  total_passengers
46 1960-09-30              1736
42 1959-09-30              1570
45 1960-06-30              1468
38 1958-09-30              1400
34 1957-09-30              1336



The annual totals from resample("YE") are identical to those from groupby(flights["date"].dt.year). The two approaches differ in syntax and flexibility — resample() is more natural for regularly spaced time series and integrates directly with the datetime index, while groupby() with .dt components works with any DataFrame regardless of whether the date is the index.
The highest single-quarter total occurs in Q3 of the final years of the dataset, reflecting both the summer peak and the overall upward trend in passenger numbers throughout the 1950s.
The highest year-on-year growth rate occurs in the early years of the dataset, when the base was low and commercial aviation was expanding rapidly.


# 4. Year-on-year growth rate
annual["pct_change"] = annual["total_passengers"].pct_change() * 100
print(annual[["date", "total_passengers", "pct_change"]])

         date  total_passengers  pct_change
0  1949-12-31              1520         NaN
1  1950-12-31              1676   10.263158
2  1951-12-31              2042   21.837709
3  1952-12-31              2364   15.768854
4  1953-12-31              2700   14.213198
5  1954-12-31              2867    6.185185
6  1955-12-31              3408   18.869899
7  1956-12-31              3939   15.580986
8  1957-12-31              4421   12.236608
9  1958-12-31              4572    3.415517
10 1959-12-31              5140   12.423447
11 1960-12-31              5714   11.167315



# 5. Upsample to daily resolution with asfreq()
daily = flights["passengers"].resample("D").asfreq()
print(daily.isna().sum())
print(daily.head(10))

4209
date
1949-01-01    112.0
1949-01-02      NaN
1949-01-03      NaN
1949-01-04      NaN
1949-01-05      NaN
1949-01-06      NaN
1949-01-07      NaN
1949-01-08      NaN
1949-01-09      NaN
1949-01-10      NaN
Freq: D, Name: passengers, dtype: float64



# 6. Spread monthly totals evenly across days
daily_filled = flights["passengers"].resample("D").ffill()
days_in_month = daily_filled.index.days_in_month
daily_estimate = (daily_filled / days_in_month).round(2)
print(daily_estimate.head(35))

date
1949-01-01    3.61
1949-01-02    3.61
1949-01-03    3.61
1949-01-04    3.61
1949-01-05    3.61
1949-01-06    3.61
1949-01-07    3.61
1949-01-08    3.61
1949-01-09    3.61
1949-01-10    3.61
1949-01-11    3.61
1949-01-12    3.61
1949-01-13    3.61
1949-01-14    3.61
1949-01-15    3.61
1949-01-16    3.61
1949-01-17    3.61
1949-01-18    3.61
1949-01-19    3.61
1949-01-20    3.61
1949-01-21    3.61
1949-01-22    3.61
1949-01-23    3.61
1949-01-24    3.61
1949-01-25    3.61
1949-01-26    3.61
1949-01-27    3.61
1949-01-28    3.61
1949-01-29    3.61
1949-01-30    3.61
1949-01-31    3.61
1949-02-01    4.21
1949-02-02    4.21
1949-02-03    4.21
1949-02-04    4.21
Freq: D, dtype: float64



Upsampling with .asfreq() creates a row for every single day between the first and last date in the index, but only the original month-start dates have a value — every other day is NaN. This is the gap-filling behavior in action: resample() builds its date range from the calendar, not from the rows that happen to exist, so it makes the resolution mismatch between the original data and the daily target explicit rather than silently ignoring it. groupby() could not do this at all, since there is nothing in the original data to group by day.
.ffill() propagates each month’s total forward to every day until the next known value, so every day in a given month temporarily holds that month’s total. Dividing by .dt.days_in_month then converts each day’s value from “the whole month’s total” to “this day’s share of the total,” correctly accounting for the fact that months have different lengths (28–31 days). The result is a daily series whose values sum back up to the original monthly totals — a reasonable estimate given the assumption of even distribution, even though the true day-to-day pattern (e.g. weekday vs weekend travel) is not recoverable from monthly data alone.





Exercise 3.11.10
This exercise is a complete wrangling workflow on the gapminder dataset — a dataset covering GDP per capita, life expectancy, and population for countries around the world from 1952 to 2007. It mirrors the structure of the Topic 3 capstone but on a dataset you have not used before.
import pandas as pd

url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/gapminderDataFiveYear.csv"
gapminder = pd.read_csv(url)
Work through the following steps:

Inspect the dataset. How many rows and columns does it have? What are the column names and dtypes? Are there any missing values?
Standardise the column names to snake_case (all lowercase, spaces replaced with underscores).
Filter to the years 1952 and 2007 only. Create two separate DataFrames — gapminder_1952 and gapminder_2007 — and merge them on country and continent to get both years’ values side by side. Use suffixes _1952 and _2007 to distinguish the columns.
Compute two new columns on the merged DataFrame:

life_exp_growth: the change in life expectancy from 1952 to 2007
gdp_growth: the percentage change in GDP per capita from 1952 to 2007

Summarise by continent: compute the mean life_exp_growth and mean gdp_growth for each continent. Sort by life_exp_growth in descending order.
Identify the top 5 and bottom 5 countries by life_exp_growth. Do the countries with the greatest improvements also tend to have the highest GDP growth?



Solution


import pandas as pd

url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/plotly/datasets/master/gapminderDataFiveYear.csv"
gapminder = pd.read_csv(url)

# 1. Inspect
print(gapminder.shape)
print(gapminder.dtypes)
print(gapminder.isna().sum())

(1704, 6)
country          str
year           int64
pop          float64
continent        str
lifeExp      float64
gdpPercap    float64
dtype: object
country      0
year         0
pop          0
continent    0
lifeExp      0
gdpPercap    0
dtype: int64



# 2. Standardise column names
gapminder.columns = [col.lower().replace(" ", "_") for col in gapminder.columns]
print(gapminder.columns.tolist())

['country', 'year', 'pop', 'continent', 'lifeexp', 'gdppercap']



# 3. Filter to 1952 and 2007 and merge
gapminder_1952 = gapminder[gapminder["year"] == 1952].copy()
gapminder_2007 = gapminder[gapminder["year"] == 2007].copy()

merged = gapminder_1952.merge(
    gapminder_2007,
    on=["country", "continent"],
    suffixes=("_1952", "_2007")
)
print(merged.shape)
print(merged.head())

(142, 10)
       country  year_1952    pop_1952 continent  lifeexp_1952  gdppercap_1952  \
0  Afghanistan       1952   8425333.0      Asia        28.801      779.445314   
1      Albania       1952   1282697.0    Europe        55.230     1601.056136   
2      Algeria       1952   9279525.0    Africa        43.077     2449.008185   
3       Angola       1952   4232095.0    Africa        30.015     3520.610273   
4    Argentina       1952  17876956.0  Americas        62.485     5911.315053   

   year_2007    pop_2007  lifeexp_2007  gdppercap_2007  
0       2007  31889923.0        43.828      974.580338  
1       2007   3600523.0        76.423     5937.029526  
2       2007  33333216.0        72.301     6223.367465  
3       2007  12420476.0        42.731     4797.231267  
4       2007  40301927.0        75.320    12779.379640  



# 4. Compute growth columns
merged["life_exp_growth"] = (
    merged["lifeexp_2007"] - merged["lifeexp_1952"]
).round(2)

merged["gdp_growth"] = (
    (merged["gdppercap_2007"] - merged["gdppercap_1952"])
    / merged["gdppercap_1952"] * 100
).round(1)


# 5. Summarise by continent
print(
    merged.groupby("continent")
    .agg(
        mean_life_exp_growth=("life_exp_growth", "mean"),
        mean_gdp_growth=("gdp_growth", "mean")
    )
    .round(2)
    .sort_values("mean_life_exp_growth", ascending=False)
)

           mean_life_exp_growth  mean_gdp_growth
continent                                       
Asia                      24.41           514.56
Americas                  20.33           176.28
Africa                    15.67           178.29
Europe                    13.24           372.42
Oceania                   11.46           190.80



# 6. Top 5 and bottom 5 countries by life expectancy growth
print("Top 5:")
print(
    merged.sort_values("life_exp_growth", ascending=False)
    [["country", "continent", "life_exp_growth", "gdp_growth"]]
    .head(5)
)

print("\nBottom 5:")
print(
    merged.sort_values("life_exp_growth")
    [["country", "continent", "life_exp_growth", "gdp_growth"]]
    .head(5)
)

Top 5:
          country continent  life_exp_growth  gdp_growth
96           Oman      Asia            38.06      1120.6
137       Vietnam      Asia            33.84       303.5
59      Indonesia      Asia            33.18       372.3
109  Saudi Arabia      Asia            32.90       235.2
75          Libya    Africa            31.23       405.0

Bottom 5:
       country continent  life_exp_growth  gdp_growth
141   Zimbabwe    Africa            -4.96        15.4
121  Swaziland    Africa            -1.79       293.0
140     Zambia    Africa             0.35        10.8
73     Lesotho    Africa             0.45       425.1
13    Botswana    Africa             3.11      1376.6


Task 1: The dataset has 1704 rows and 6 columns with no missing values. Each row represents a country-year observation.
Task 5: Asia shows the greatest mean improvement in both life expectancy and GDP — driven by countries like Japan, South Korea, and China. Europe shows more modest gains because life expectancy and GDP were already high in 1952.
Task 6: The countries with the greatest life expectancy improvements are not always those with the highest GDP growth — reflecting that improvements in public health, sanitation, and medicine can drive life expectancy gains independently of economic growth. The bottom 5 countries tend to be those affected by conflict or epidemics, which caused life expectancy to stagnate or fall in parts of sub-Saharan Africa.




Exercise 3.11.11
This exercise is a multi-step data wrangling challenge that combines joining, string manipulation, missing value handling, and groupby on the Titanic data. The Name column contains more information than it first appears — last name, title, and for married women, a maiden name in parentheses — and extracting these cleanly requires some careful string work.
Load both Titanic datasets from GitHub and work through the steps below.
import pandas as pd

url_core  = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/data/titanic_core.csv"
url_names = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/data/titanic_names.csv"

core  = pd.read_csv(url_core)
names = pd.read_csv(url_names)

print(names["Name"].head(10).tolist())

Prepare names for merging: rename PassengerId, Pclass, and Sex to passenger_id, pclass, and sex. Then merge the two DataFrames on all three shared keys using a left join. Verify the row count is 891.
The Name column follows the format "Last, Title. Rest" (e.g. "Braund, Mr. Owen Harris"). Use the .str accessor to extract:

last_name: everything before the comma
title: the word between the comma-space and the following period

Use .value_counts() on title. You will see a long tail of rare titles ("Don", "Lady", "Capt", and so on). Create a simplified title_group column using .replace() or np.select() with the following mapping:

"Mr" → "Mr"
"Miss" and "Mrs" → "Ms"
All other titles → "Other"

Use the last_name column to answer two questions:

Which starting letter is the most common among last names?
What is the most common last name length, measured in number of characters? Compute the length of each last name, then find the most frequent value.

Compute the survival rate for each combination of title_group and pclass. Which title-class combination had the highest survival rate?



Hint

For step 2, recall the pattern from earlier exercises: splitting the name on ", " isolates the part starting with the title, and splitting that result on "." extracts just the title word. For last_name, you only need a single split on "," and take the first part.
For step 3, .replace() takes a dictionary mapping old values to new values, for example {"Don": "Other", "Lady": "Other"}. Rather than typing out every rare title individually, you can build this dictionary automatically using a dictionary comprehension over the titles you want to group as "Other":
{t: "Other" for t in merged["title"].unique() if t not in ["Mr", "Miss", "Mrs"]}



Solution


import pandas as pd

url_core  = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/data/titanic_core.csv"
url_names = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/data/titanic_names.csv"

core  = pd.read_csv(url_core)
names = pd.read_csv(url_names)

# 1. Prepare and merge
names = names.rename(columns={
    "PassengerId": "passenger_id",
    "Pclass":      "pclass",
    "Sex":         "sex"
})
merged = core.merge(names, on=["passenger_id", "pclass", "sex"], how="left")
print("Rows:", len(merged))

Rows: 891



# 2. Extract last_name and title
merged["last_name"] = merged["Name"].str.split(",").str[0]
merged["title"] = (
    merged["Name"].str.split(", ").str[1]
    .str.split(".").str[0]
)
print(merged[["Name", "last_name", "title"]].head())

                                                Name  last_name title
0                            Braund, Mr. Owen Harris     Braund    Mr
1  Cumings, Mrs. John Bradley (Florence Briggs Th...    Cumings   Mrs
2                             Heikkinen, Miss. Laina  Heikkinen  Miss
3       Futrelle, Mrs. Jacques Heath (Lily May Peel)   Futrelle   Mrs
4                           Allen, Mr. William Henry      Allen    Mr



# 3. Simplified title group
merged["title_group"] = merged["title"].replace(
    {t: "Other" for t in merged["title"].unique()
     if t not in ["Mr", "Miss", "Mrs"]}
)
merged["title_group"] = merged["title_group"].replace({"Miss": "Ms", "Mrs": "Ms"})
print(merged["title_group"].value_counts())

title_group
Mr       517
Ms       307
Other     67
Name: count, dtype: int64



# 4. Most common starting letter and length of last names
print(merged["last_name"].str[0].value_counts().head())

last_name
S    86
M    74
B    72
C    69
H    69
Name: count, dtype: int64



print(merged["last_name"].str.len().value_counts().head())

last_name
6    207
7    193
5    130
8    103
4     92
Name: count, dtype: int64



# 5. Survival rate by title group and class
print(
    merged.groupby(["title_group", "pclass"])["survived"]
    .mean()
    .round(3)
    .sort_values(ascending=False)
)

title_group  pclass
Ms           1         0.966
             2         0.920
Other        1         0.667
             2         0.556
Ms           3         0.500
Other        3         0.393
Mr           1         0.346
             3         0.113
             2         0.088
Name: survived, dtype: float64


Task 2: Splitting on ", " first isolates the part of the string starting with the title, and splitting that on "." extracts just the title word. This relies on the title always being the second comma-separated segment in the name, which holds for every row in this dataset.
Task 4: The most common starting letter for last names is “S”, followed closely by “M” and “B” — a pattern that broadly reflects the most common surname initials among the British and Northern European passengers who make up most of the dataset. The most common last name length is 6 or 7 characters, which is typical for English-language surnames; very short (3-4 character) and very long (10+ character) names are comparatively rare.
Task 5: First-class “Ms” (Miss/Mrs) passengers had the highest survival rate — consistent with the “women and children first” pattern combined with the class advantage.



Exercise 3.11.12
This exercise practices importing and combining multiple files with a for loop.
The data comes from FRED (Federal Reserve Economic Data) and is split into one CSV file per decade, each containing the same five monthly variables. The CPI column is the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers, indexed so that the average over 1982–1984 equals 100. The monthly csv files can be found in this Github folder.
import pandas as pd

base_url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/data/FRED/"
decades = [1950, 1960, 1970, 1980, 1990, 2000, 2010]

Use a for loop over decades to build the file name for each decade, load it from base_url, and store each resulting DataFrame in a list called fred_dfs. Print the shape of each DataFrame as you load it.
Use pd.concat() to combine the list into a single DataFrame called fred. Reset the index, convert the DATE column to datetime and sort the data chronologically. Print the shape of the combined DataFrame and confirm it covers 1950 through 2019.
Compute the mean CPI for each year using groupby. Then compute the year-on-year percentage change in this annual mean using .pct_change() — this gives you the annual inflation rate. Multiply by 100 and round to one decimal place.
Identify the 5 years with the highest inflation rate and the 5 years with the lowest (most negative) inflation rate. What historical periods do these correspond to?
Compute the mean annual inflation rate for each decade. Which decade had the highest average inflation?



Hint

For step 1, build each URL inside the loop using an f-string, for example base_url + f"FRED_monthly_{decade}.csv", then pass it directly to pd.read_csv(). Remember to use .append() to add each DataFrame to fred_dfs.
For step 5, you can extract the decade from the year using integer arithmetic: (year // 10) * 10 rounds any year down to its decade, for example 1987 becomes 1980.



Solution


import pandas as pd

base_url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/data/FRED/"
decades = [1950, 1960, 1970, 1980, 1990, 2000, 2010]

# 1. Import each decade's file in a loop
fred_dfs = []

for decade in decades:
    url = base_url + f"FRED_monthly_{decade}.csv"
    df = pd.read_csv(url)
    fred_dfs.append(df)
    print(decade, df.shape)

1950 (120, 6)
1960 (120, 6)
1970 (120, 6)
1980 (120, 6)
1990 (120, 6)
2000 (120, 6)
2010 (120, 6)



# 2. Combine, convert, and sort
fred = pd.concat(fred_dfs).reset_index(drop=True)
fred["DATE"] = pd.to_datetime(fred["DATE"])
fred = fred.sort_values("DATE").reset_index(drop=True)

print(fred.shape)
print(fred["DATE"].min(), "to", fred["DATE"].max())

(840, 6)
1950-01-01 00:00:00 to 2019-12-01 00:00:00



# 3. Annual mean CPI and year-on-year inflation rate
annual_cpi = fred.groupby(fred["DATE"].dt.year)["CPI"].mean()
inflation = (annual_cpi.pct_change() * 100).round(1)
print(inflation.head(10))

DATE
1950    NaN
1951    8.0
1952    2.3
1953    0.8
1954    0.3
1955   -0.2
1956    1.5
1957    3.4
1958    2.7
1959    1.0
Name: CPI, dtype: float64



# 4. Highest and lowest inflation years
print("Highest inflation years:")
print(inflation.sort_values(ascending=False).head(5))

print("\nLowest inflation years:")
print(inflation.sort_values().head(5))

Highest inflation years:
DATE
1980    13.5
1979    11.3
1974    11.0
1981    10.4
1975     9.1
Name: CPI, dtype: float64

Lowest inflation years:
DATE
2009   -0.3
1955   -0.2
2015    0.1
1954    0.3
1953    0.8
Name: CPI, dtype: float64



# 5. Mean inflation rate by decade
decade_of_year = (inflation.index // 10) * 10
print(inflation.groupby(decade_of_year).mean().round(2))

DATE
1950    2.20
1960    2.33
1970    7.10
1980    5.56
1990    2.99
2000    2.58
2010    1.76
Name: CPI, dtype: float64



The highest inflation years cluster in the mid-to-late 1970s and around 1980 — the period of the oil price shocks and the resulting stagflation, when annual inflation exceeded 10%. The lowest (most negative) values appear around 2009, reflecting the brief period of deflation during the global financial crisis, when prices fell rather than rose.
The 1970s has by far the highest average inflation rate of any decade in the data, consistent with the well-known “Great Inflation” period in US economic history. The 1950s and 2010s show the lowest average inflation, reflecting relatively stable price growth in both the early postwar period and the decade following the 2008 financial crisis.

title: How matplotlib works
text:
Exercises
We introduce the two building blocks of every matplotlib figure — the Figure and the Axes — and establish the object-oriented coding pattern we will follow throughout this topic: create, draw, label, show.

title: How matplotlib works
text:
Exercise 4.1.1
Use np.linspace() to generate 50 evenly spaced values between 0 and 10 for the x-axis. Compute y = x ** 2 for the corresponding y-values.
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

Create a Figure and Axes using plt.subplots() with figsize=(7, 5).
Plot x against y using ax.plot().
Add an x-axis label, a y-axis label, and a title of your choice.
Call plt.tight_layout() and plt.show() to display the finished chart.



Solution


import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.linspace(0, 10, 50)
y = x ** 2

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))

ax.plot(x, y)

ax.set_xlabel("x")
ax.set_ylabel("y")
ax.set_title("y = x squared")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








This is the basic pattern you will use for every chart in this topic: create the Figure and Axes, draw on the Axes, label it, then display it.




Exercise 4.1.2
This exercise is about understanding what the Figure controls versus what the Axes controls. Run the two code blocks below and compare the results.
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.arange(0, 10)
y = x * 2

# Block A
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4, 3))
ax.plot(x, y)
ax.set_title("Small figure")
plt.show()

# Block B
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
ax.plot(x, y)
ax.set_title("Large figure")
plt.show()

What changes between Block A and Block B? Which object — fig or ax — is responsible for this change?
Without changing figsize, how would you make the title larger? Which object would you call a method on to do this?
In your own words, explain the difference between what the Figure controls and what the Axes controls.



Solution


import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.arange(0, 10)
y = x * 2

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4, 3))
ax.plot(x, y)
ax.set_title("Small figure")
plt.show()









fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
ax.plot(x, y)
ax.set_title("Large figure")
plt.show()









The overall size of the image changes — Block B is visibly larger than Block A. This is controlled by figsize=, which is passed to plt.subplots() when creating the Figure. This confirms that overall size is a Figure-level property.
You would call ax.set_title("...", fontsize=20) — font size is a property of the text drawn on the Axes, not of the Figure as a whole.
The Figure is the outer canvas — it controls things like overall size and can hold multiple Axes panels. The Axes is the individual plot panel — it controls the data being drawn, the axis labels, the title, and tick marks. Most of the customisation you will do in this topic happens on the Axes, not the Figure.





Exercise 4.1.3
Use np.arange() to generate two simple sequences and plot them as two separate lines on the same Axes.
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.arange(0, 20)
y1 = x
y2 = x ** 0.5 * 3

Create a Figure and Axes with figsize=(8, 5).
Call ax.plot() twice on the same ax — once for y1 with the label "Linear", once for y2 with the label "Square root".
Add axis labels, a title, and call ax.legend() to display the labels you set in step 2.
Which line grows faster as x increases? At what point (roughly) do the two lines cross?



Solution


import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.arange(0, 20)
y1 = x
y2 = x ** 0.5 * 3

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))

ax.plot(x, y1, label="Linear")
ax.plot(x, y2, label="Square root")

ax.set_xlabel("x")
ax.set_ylabel("y")
ax.set_title("Linear vs square root growth")
ax.legend()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









The linear line grows faster once x is large enough — the two lines cross somewhere around x = 9. Before that point, the square root line is higher. Calling ax.plot() more than once on the same ax draws multiple lines on the same panel, which is exactly how you will build charts with several lines later in this topic.





Exercise 4.1.4
Run the code below and answer the questions that follow.
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

years = np.arange(2015, 2025)
revenue = [12, 15, 14, 18, 22, 20, 19, 25, 30, 34]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))

ax.plot(years, revenue)

ax.set_xlabel("Year")
ax.set_ylabel("Revenue (millions USD)")
ax.set_title("Company revenue, 2015–2024")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

What does the x-axis represent? What does the y-axis represent?
In which year did revenue decrease compared to the previous year? How many times does this happen across the whole period?
Based on the chart alone, would you describe the overall trend as increasing, decreasing, or roughly flat?



Solution


import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

years = np.arange(2015, 2025)
revenue = [12, 15, 14, 18, 22, 20, 19, 25, 30, 34]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))

ax.plot(years, revenue)

ax.set_xlabel("Year")
ax.set_ylabel("Revenue (millions USD)")
ax.set_title("Company revenue, 2015–2024")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









The x-axis represents the year, from 2015 to 2024. The y-axis represents the company’s revenue in millions of US dollars.
Revenue decreases in 2017 (compared to 2016) and again in 2021 (compared to 2020) — two decreases in total. Reading these directly off a chart like this, rather than from a table of numbers, is exactly what line charts are good at: spotting a dip is much faster visually than scanning a column of numbers.
The overall trend is clearly increasing — revenue roughly triples over the period, despite the two temporary dips.





Exercise 4.1.5
The small DataFrame below records the average monthly temperature in Bergen, Norway, across one year.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

bergen = pd.DataFrame({
    "month":       ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun",
                    "Jul", "Aug", "Sep", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec"],
    "month_num":   range(1, 13),
    "temp_c":      [2.1, 2.3, 4.0, 6.8, 11.2, 14.1,
                    15.8, 15.5, 12.3, 8.6, 4.9, 2.8]
})

Create a Figure and Axes with figsize=(9, 5).
Plot bergen["month_num"] on the x-axis and bergen["temp_c"] on the y-axis using ax.plot().
Add an x-axis label, a y-axis label with the correct unit, and a title.
In which month is the average temperature highest? In which month is it lowest?



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

bergen = pd.DataFrame({
    "month":       ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun",
                    "Jul", "Aug", "Sep", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec"],
    "month_num":   range(1, 13),
    "temp_c":      [2.1, 2.3, 4.0, 6.8, 11.2, 14.1,
                    15.8, 15.5, 12.3, 8.6, 4.9, 2.8]
})

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))

ax.plot(bergen["month_num"], bergen["temp_c"])

ax.set_xlabel("Month")
ax.set_ylabel("Average temperature (°C)")
ax.set_title("Average monthly temperature in Bergen")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









The highest average temperature is in July (15.8°C), and the lowest is in January (2.1°C). Note that ax.plot() accepts DataFrame columns directly — there is no need to convert them to plain Python lists or NumPy arrays first.

title: Reshaping data
text:
Exercises
We cover two reshaping operations — pivot() to convert long data to wide format, and melt() to go back the other way — since the shape of a DataFrame directly affects how easy it is to plot.

title: Reshaping data
text:
Exercise 4.2.1
For each of the three small DataFrames below, decide whether it is in wide or long format, and explain your reasoning in one sentence.
import pandas as pd

df_a = pd.DataFrame({
    "country": ["Norway", "Norway", "Sweden", "Sweden"],
    "year":    [2000, 2010, 2000, 2010],
    "gdp":     [36000, 60000, 34000, 52000]
})

df_b = pd.DataFrame({
    "country": ["Norway", "Sweden"],
    "gdp_2000": [36000, 34000],
    "gdp_2010": [60000, 52000]
})

df_c = pd.DataFrame({
    "student": ["Alice", "Alice", "Bob", "Bob"],
    "subject": ["Math", "English", "Math", "English"],
    "score":   [88, 76, 91, 82]
})


Solution

df_a is long — each row is a single country-year observation, and year appears as values within a column rather than as separate columns.
df_b is wide — each row is a single country, and each year has its own column (gdp_2000, gdp_2010). The same information as df_a, but reshaped.
df_c is long — each row is a single student-subject observation. Reshaping this to wide would produce one row per student, with Math and English as separate columns.
The general test: in long format, a variable like year or subject is stored as values inside a column. In wide format, those same values become the column headers themselves.




Exercise 4.2.2
The flights dataset is a good example of data that is about as long as it can be — 144 rows, one per month per year, with a single passengers column.
import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")
flights.head()

Use .pivot() to reshape flights to wide format, with year as the row index, month as the columns, and passengers as the values. Assign the result to flights_wide.
What is the shape of flights_wide? How does it compare to the shape of flights, and why?
The columns of flights_wide are not in calendar order (January to December). Use the following list to reorder them: ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun", "Jul", "Aug", "Sep", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec"]



Hint

For step 3, you can select columns in a specific order by passing a list of column names inside square brackets, e.g. flights_wide[my_ordered_list].



Solution


import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")

# 1. Pivot to wide
flights_wide = flights.pivot(index="year", columns="month", values="passengers")
print(flights_wide.head())

month  Apr  Aug  Dec  Feb  Jan  Jul  Jun  Mar  May  Nov  Oct  Sep
year                                                             
1949   129  148  118  118  112  148  135  132  121  104  119  136
1950   135  170  140  126  115  170  149  141  125  114  133  158
1951   163  199  166  150  145  199  178  178  172  146  162  184
1952   181  242  194  180  171  230  218  193  183  172  191  209
1953   235  272  201  196  196  264  243  236  229  180  211  237



# 2. Shape comparison
print("Long shape:", flights.shape)
print("Wide shape:", flights_wide.shape)

Long shape: (144, 3)
Wide shape: (12, 12)



# 3. Reorder columns chronologically
month_order = ["Jan", "Feb", "Mar", "Apr", "May", "Jun",
               "Jul", "Aug", "Sep", "Oct", "Nov", "Dec"]
flights_wide = flights_wide[month_order]
print(flights_wide.head())

month  Jan  Feb  Mar  Apr  May  Jun  Jul  Aug  Sep  Oct  Nov  Dec
year                                                             
1949   112  118  132  129  121  135  148  148  136  119  104  118
1950   115  126  141  135  125  149  170  170  158  133  114  140
1951   145  150  178  163  172  178  199  199  184  162  146  166
1952   171  180  193  181  183  218  230  242  209  191  172  194
1953   196  196  236  235  229  243  264  272  237  211  180  201



flights has 144 rows and 3 columns. flights_wide has 12 rows (one per year) and 12 columns (one per month) — the same 144 values, just rearranged into a grid instead of a long list. This is about as compact as this data can get: every month-year combination now has its own cell rather than its own row.
.pivot() sorts the new columns alphabetically by default, which is why the months came out in the wrong order — this is a common surprise the first time you pivot on a text column like month names.





Exercise 4.2.3
Continue with flights_wide from the previous exercise, or recreate it if needed. This exercise reshapes it back to long format and checks that nothing was lost in the process.
import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")
flights_wide = flights.pivot(index="year", columns="month", values="passengers")

.melt() expects the row index to be a regular column rather than the DataFrame’s index. Use .reset_index() on flights_wide first, and inspect the result.
Use .melt() to reshape the result of step 1 back to long format, with year as id_vars, and set var_name="month" and value_name="passengers". Assign the result to flights_long.
Compare flights_long to the original flights DataFrame: do they have the same number of rows? Sort both by year and month and compare the total passenger count in each — do they match?



Solution


import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")
flights_wide = flights.pivot(index="year", columns="month", values="passengers")

# 1. Reset index
flights_wide_reset = flights_wide.reset_index()
print(flights_wide_reset.head())

month  year  Apr  Aug  Dec  Feb  Jan  Jul  Jun  Mar  May  Nov  Oct  Sep
0      1949  129  148  118  118  112  148  135  132  121  104  119  136
1      1950  135  170  140  126  115  170  149  141  125  114  133  158
2      1951  163  199  166  150  145  199  178  178  172  146  162  184
3      1952  181  242  194  180  171  230  218  193  183  172  191  209
4      1953  235  272  201  196  196  264  243  236  229  180  211  237



# 2. Melt back to long
flights_long = flights_wide_reset.melt(
    id_vars="year", var_name="month", value_name="passengers"
)
print(flights_long.head())

   year month  passengers
0  1949   Apr         129
1  1950   Apr         135
2  1951   Apr         163
3  1952   Apr         181
4  1953   Apr         235



# 3. Compare row counts and totals
print("Original rows:", len(flights))
print("Reshaped rows:", len(flights_long))
print("Original total passengers:", flights["passengers"].sum())
print("Reshaped total passengers:", flights_long["passengers"].sum())

Original rows: 144
Reshaped rows: 144
Original total passengers: 40363
Reshaped total passengers: 40363



Both DataFrames have 144 rows and the same total passenger count, confirming that .pivot() followed by .melt() is a true round trip — no information was lost, only the shape changed. The row order may differ from the original since .melt() processes column by column, but the set of values is identical.





Exercise 4.2.4
Load the full gapminder dataset and practice pivoting on a larger subset than shown in the video, using a different values= column each time.
import pandas as pd

gapminder = pd.read_csv("data/gapminder.csv")
print(gapminder.shape)
gapminder.head()

Filter gapminder to just the continent "Europe". Pivot this subset to wide format with country as the index, year as the columns, and lifeExp as the values. How many rows and columns does the result have?
Repeat the same pivot, but this time use gdpPercap as the values instead of lifeExp. What changed between this result and the one in step 1, and what stayed the same?
Why can index=, columns=, and values= be treated as three independent choices rather than a fixed template? Give an example of a different valid combination of the three arguments using the gapminder columns.



Solution


import pandas as pd

gapminder = pd.read_csv("data/gapminder.csv")

# 1. Pivot Europe on lifeExp
europe = gapminder[gapminder["continent"] == "Europe"]
europe_life_wide = europe.pivot(index="country", columns="year", values="lifeExp")
print(europe_life_wide.shape)
print(europe_life_wide.head())

The row and column structure — which countries, which years — is identical in both results, since index= and columns= were unchanged. What changed is the values filling the grid: life expectancy figures in step 1, GDP per capita figures in step 2.
index=, columns=, and values= each independently answer a different question: what should identify the rows, what should identify the columns, and what number should fill each cell. Any column not used as index= or columns= can be used as values=, provided the index-columns combination uniquely identifies each cell. For example, index="year", columns="country", values="pop" would produce a table of population figures with years as rows and countries as columns instead.





Exercise 4.2.5
When reshaping data, you sometimes want to keep more than one identifying column intact. This exercise practices that using country and continent together.
import pandas as pd

gapminder = pd.read_csv("data/gapminder.csv")

subset = gapminder[
    gapminder["country"].isin(["Norway", "Sweden", "Denmark", "Egypt", "Kenya"])
]

Pivot subset to wide format using both ["country", "continent"] as the index, year as the columns, and lifeExp as the values. What does the resulting index look like?
Reset the index so country and continent become regular columns again.
Melt the result of step 2 back to long format, using both ["country", "continent"] as id_vars. Verify the result has one row per country-year combination, with continent correctly preserved for every row.



Solution


import pandas as pd

gapminder = pd.read_csv("data/gapminder.csv")

subset = gapminder[
    gapminder["country"].isin(["Norway", "Sweden", "Denmark", "Egypt", "Kenya"])
]

# 1. Pivot with two identifier columns
wide = subset.pivot(index=["country", "continent"], columns="year", values="lifeExp")
print(wide.head())

year                 1952    1957    1962    1967    1972    1977    1982  \
country continent                                                           
Denmark Europe     70.780  71.810  72.350  72.960  73.470  74.690  74.630   
Egypt   Africa     41.893  44.444  46.992  49.293  51.137  53.319  56.006   
Kenya   Africa     42.270  44.686  47.949  50.654  53.559  56.155  58.766   
Norway  Europe     72.670  73.440  73.470  74.080  74.340  75.370  75.970   
Sweden  Europe     71.860  72.490  73.370  74.160  74.720  75.440  76.420   

year                 1987    1992    1997    2002    2007  
country continent                                          
Denmark Europe     74.800  75.330  76.110  77.180  78.332  
Egypt   Africa     59.797  63.674  67.217  69.806  71.338  
Kenya   Africa     59.339  59.285  54.407  50.992  54.110  
Norway  Europe     75.890  77.320  78.320  79.050  80.196  
Sweden  Europe     77.190  78.160  79.390  80.040  80.884  


Passing a list to index= creates a two-level row index — each row is identified by the combination of country and continent together, rather than by country alone. This is useful because continent would otherwise be lost during the pivot, since it is neither the index, the columns, nor the values.

# 2. Reset index
wide = wide.reset_index()
print(wide.head())

year  country continent    1952    1957    1962    1967    1972    1977  \
0     Denmark    Europe  70.780  71.810  72.350  72.960  73.470  74.690   
1       Egypt    Africa  41.893  44.444  46.992  49.293  51.137  53.319   
2       Kenya    Africa  42.270  44.686  47.949  50.654  53.559  56.155   
3      Norway    Europe  72.670  73.440  73.470  74.080  74.340  75.370   
4      Sweden    Europe  71.860  72.490  73.370  74.160  74.720  75.440   

year    1982    1987    1992    1997    2002    2007  
0     74.630  74.800  75.330  76.110  77.180  78.332  
1     56.006  59.797  63.674  67.217  69.806  71.338  
2     58.766  59.339  59.285  54.407  50.992  54.110  
3     75.970  75.890  77.320  78.320  79.050  80.196  
4     76.420  77.190  78.160  79.390  80.040  80.884  



# 3. Melt back with two id_vars
long = wide.melt(
    id_vars=["country", "continent"], var_name="year", value_name="lifeExp"
)
print(long.sort_values(["country", "year"]).head(10))

    country continent  year  lifeExp
0   Denmark    Europe  1952    70.78
5   Denmark    Europe  1957    71.81
10  Denmark    Europe  1962    72.35
15  Denmark    Europe  1967    72.96
20  Denmark    Europe  1972    73.47
25  Denmark    Europe  1977    74.69
30  Denmark    Europe  1982    74.63
35  Denmark    Europe  1987    74.80
40  Denmark    Europe  1992    75.33
45  Denmark    Europe  1997    76.11


The melted result has one row per country-year combination, and every row correctly retains its continent value. Passing a list to id_vars= works the same way as passing a list to index= in .pivot() — both let you carry more than one identifying column through the reshape.

title: Line charts
text:
Exercises
We cover line charts for showing how a numeric variable changes over time.

title: Line charts
text:
Exercise 4.3.1
Use the healthexp dataset to practice the basic single-line pattern with a new dataset.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv")

Filter healthexp to a single country of your choice and assign the result to a variable.
Create a Figure and Axes with figsize=(9, 5).
Plot Year on the x-axis and Life_Expectancy on the y-axis.
Add an x-axis label, a y-axis label, and a title that includes the name of the country you chose.



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv")

germany = healthexp[healthexp["Country"] == "Germany"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))

ax.plot(germany["Year"], germany["Life_Expectancy"])

ax.set_xlabel("Year")
ax.set_ylabel("Life expectancy (years)")
ax.set_title("Life expectancy over time in Germay")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








This is the same basic recipe from the previous videos: filter the data down to a single series, then plot it. The line chart makes the upward trend in life expectancy immediately visible, which would be much harder to spot from a table of numbers alone.




Exercise 4.3.2
Practice the loop-over-groups pattern on the healthexp dataset using a different grouping variable than the video — individual countries rather than continents.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv")
countries = ["Germany", "Japan", "USA", "Great Britain"]

Filter healthexp to just the four countries listed above.
Create a Figure and Axes with figsize=(9, 5).
Use a for loop over countries to plot each country’s life expectancy over time as its own line, using label= to set the country name.
Add axis labels, a title, and call ax.legend() to display the labels.
Which country has the highest life expectancy at the end of the period? Which had the lowest at the start?



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv")
countries = ["Germany", "Japan", "USA", "Great Britain"]

subset = healthexp[healthexp["Country"].isin(countries)]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))

for country in countries:
    group = subset[subset["Country"] == country]
    ax.plot(group["Year"], group["Life_Expectancy"], label=country)

ax.set_xlabel("Year")
ax.set_ylabel("Life expectancy (years)")
ax.set_title("Life expectancy over time by country")
ax.legend(title="Country")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









Japan both starts and ends the period with the highest life expectancy. The USA has the lowest life expectancy at the end of the period among these four countries, despite the highest spending.





Exercise 4.3.3
The file below contains quarterly data US GDP. Load it and see what happens when you plot a date column that has not been converted to datetime.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/data/FRED/GDP.csv"
gdp = pd.read_csv(url)
print(gdp.dtypes)
gdp.head()

Without converting the DATE column, create a line chart of GDP over DATE. Use figsize=(12, 5). What does the x-axis look like? Is it readable?
Now convert DATE to datetime using pd.to_datetime() and create the same chart again. How does the x-axis change?
In your own words, explain why matplotlib is able to space and format the x-axis sensibly in the second chart but not the first.



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/data/FRED/GDP.csv"
gdp = pd.read_csv(url)

# 1. Plot with DATE as a string
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5))
ax.plot(gdp["DATE"], gdp["GDP"])
ax.set_title("GDP over time (DATE as string)")
plt.show()









# 2. Convert to datetime and replot
gdp["DATE"] = pd.to_datetime(gdp["DATE"])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5))
ax.plot(gdp["DATE"], gdp["GDP"])
ax.set_xlabel("Year")
ax.set_ylabel("GDP (billions USD)")
ax.set_title("US GDP over time")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









With DATE as a string, matplotlib treats every unique date as a separate category on the x-axis and tries to print every single one as a text label. With over 300 quarters in the data, the labels overlap into an unreadable smear.
Once DATE is a proper datetime, matplotlib understands that the x-axis represents time, not arbitrary categories. It can then choose sensible tick positions (e.g. one label every few years) and format them cleanly, because it knows how to compare, order, and space actual dates — something it cannot do with plain text.





Exercise 4.3.4
The DataFrame below contains a small time series that has been accidentally shuffled out of chronological order.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

sales = pd.DataFrame({
    "date":    pd.to_datetime([
        "2023-03-01", "2023-01-01", "2023-05-01", "2023-02-01",
        "2023-04-01", "2023-06-01"
    ]),
    "revenue": [42, 38, 51, 40, 47, 55]
})
sales["date"] = pd.to_datetime(sales["date"])

Plot revenue against date exactly as the data is currently ordered, using figsize=(8, 5). What does the line look like? Is this what you would expect from steadily increasing monthly revenue?
Sort sales by date and plot again. How does the chart change?
In your own words, explain why matplotlib produced a “zigzag” in step 1 even though date was already a proper datetime column.



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

sales = pd.DataFrame({
    "date":    pd.to_datetime([
        "2023-03-01", "2023-01-01", "2023-05-01", "2023-02-01",
        "2023-04-01", "2023-06-01"
    ]),
    "revenue": [42, 38, 51, 40, 47, 55]
})
sales["date"] = pd.to_datetime(sales["date"])

# 1. Plot without sorting
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.plot(sales["date"], sales["revenue"])
ax.set_title("Revenue (unsorted)")
plt.show()









# 2. Sort and replot
sales_sorted = sales.sort_values("date")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.plot(sales_sorted["date"], sales_sorted["revenue"])
ax.set_title("Revenue (sorted)")
plt.show()









ax.plot() always draws a line connecting points in the order the rows appear in the DataFrame — it does not sort by the x-values for you, even when those x-values are proper dates. Since the rows in sales were not in chronological order, the line jumps back and forth across the x-axis rather than tracing a smooth path from January to June. Having a correct datetime dtype fixes how the axis is labeled, but it does not fix the plotting order — that always depends on row order, which is why sorting is a separate and necessary step.





Exercise 4.3.5
This exercise combines data preparation, datetime conversion, and sorting into one realistic end-to-end task using US monthly macroeconomic data.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/data/FRED/FRED_monthly_2010.csv"
fred = pd.read_csv(url)
print(fred.dtypes)
fred.head()

Convert the DATE column to datetime and sort the DataFrame chronologically.
Filter the data to the years 2010 through 2015 (inclusive).
Create a line chart of UNRATE (the unemployment rate) over DATE for this filtered period, with figsize=(10, 5) and full axis labels and a title.
In which year does unemployment peak within this window? Roughly how many percentage points does it fall by the end of 2015?



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/data/FRED/FRED_monthly_2010.csv"
fred = pd.read_csv(url)

# 1. Convert and sort
fred["DATE"] = pd.to_datetime(fred["DATE"])
fred = fred.sort_values("DATE")

# 2. Filter to 2010-2015
fred_subset = fred[(fred["DATE"] >= "2010-01-01") & (fred["DATE"] <= "2015-12-01")]


# 3. Plot
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))

ax.plot(fred_subset["DATE"], fred_subset["UNRATE"])

ax.set_xlabel("Year")
ax.set_ylabel("Unemployment rate (%)")
ax.set_title("US unemployment rate, 2010–2015")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









Unemployment peaks in early 2010, close to 10%, reflecting the depth of the recovery still underway after the 2008 financial crisis. By the end of 2015 it has fallen to around 5% — a decline of roughly 4 to 5 percentage points over the six-year window, illustrating the long, gradual nature of labour market recovery after a major recession.

title: Bar charts
text:
Exercises
We cover bar charts for comparing values across discrete categories.

title: Bar charts
text:
Exercise 4.4.1
The DataFrame below is already in the right shape to be plotted directly — one row per category, with a single value to plot for each.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

sales = pd.DataFrame({
    "product": ["Coffee", "Tea", "Juice", "Water"],
    "units_sold": [420, 310, 265, 190]
})

Create a Figure and Axes with figsize=(7, 5).
Use ax.bar() to plot product on the x-axis and units_sold on the y-axis.
Add axis labels and a title.
Recreate the same chart using ax.barh() instead. What changes about which column goes on which axis?



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

sales = pd.DataFrame({
    "product": ["Coffee", "Tea", "Juice", "Water"],
    "units_sold": [420, 310, 265, 190]
})

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))

ax.bar(sales["product"], sales["units_sold"])

ax.set_xlabel("Product")
ax.set_ylabel("Units sold")
ax.set_title("Units sold by product")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









# 4. Horizontal version
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))

ax.barh(sales["product"], sales["units_sold"])

ax.set_xlabel("Units sold")
ax.set_ylabel("Product")
ax.set_title("Units sold by product")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









With ax.barh(), the categories (product) go on the y-axis and the values (units_sold) go on the x-axis — the opposite of ax.bar(). The data passed in is exactly the same; only the orientation of the bars changes.





Exercise 4.4.2
Unlike the previous exercise, real data usually needs to be summarised before it can be plotted as a bar chart. Use the titanic dataset to practice this.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")

Compute the survival rate for each passenger class using groupby.
Create a Figure and Axes with figsize=(7, 5).
Plot the result as a vertical bar chart using ax.bar().
Add axis labels and a title. What does the chart show about the relationship between passenger class and survival?



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")

survival_by_class = titanic.groupby("pclass")["survived"].mean()
print(survival_by_class)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))

ax.bar(survival_by_class.index, survival_by_class.values)

ax.set_xlabel("Passenger class")
ax.set_ylabel("Survival rate")
ax.set_title("Titanic survival rate by passenger class")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

pclass
1    0.629630
2    0.472826
3    0.242363
Name: survived, dtype: float64










Survival rate decreases clearly as passenger class number increases — first class passengers survived at the highest rate, third class at the lowest. This is the same pattern explored numerically in Topic 3, now visible at a glance.





Exercise 4.4.3
The gapminder dataset contains 142 countries — far too many to fit comfortably on a vertical bar chart.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

gapminder = pd.read_csv("data/gapminder.csv")
print(gapminder["country"].nunique())

Filter gapminder to the year 2007 and the continent "Americas".
Create a Figure and Axes with figsize=(8, 10).
Plot gdpPercap for each country as a horizontal bar chart using ax.barh(), with country on the y-axis.
Add axis labels and a title. Try recreating the same chart with ax.bar() instead of ax.barh(), keeping the same figsize. What goes wrong, and why is ax.barh() clearly the better choice for this data?



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

gapminder = pd.read_csv("data/gapminder.csv")

americas_2007 = gapminder[
    (gapminder["year"] == 2007) & (gapminder["continent"] == "Americas")
]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 10))

ax.barh(americas_2007["country"], americas_2007["gdpPercap"])

ax.set_xlabel("GDP per capita (USD)")
ax.set_ylabel("Country")
ax.set_title("GDP per capita by country, Americas, 2007")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









# Vertical version for comparison
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 10))

ax.bar(americas_2007["country"], americas_2007["gdpPercap"])

ax.set_xlabel("Country")
ax.set_ylabel("GDP per capita (USD)")
ax.set_title("GDP per capita by country, Americas, 2007")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









With ax.bar(), the country names on the x-axis overlap and become unreadable, even with a tall figsize, because there is not enough horizontal space for 25 long text labels side by side. With ax.barh(), the same labels sit comfortably on the y-axis, stacked vertically with plenty of room, since figsize height easily accommodates one row per country. As a rule of thumb, switch to horizontal bars whenever you have many categories or long category labels.





Exercise 4.4.4
This exercise builds on the previous one. The chart is readable now that it is horizontal, but the bars are still in whatever order the countries happen to appear in the data, which makes it hard to compare countries at a glance.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

gapminder = pd.read_csv("data/gapminder.csv")

americas_2007 = gapminder[
    (gapminder["year"] == 2007) & (gapminder["continent"] == "Americas")
]

Plot americas_2007 as a horizontal bar chart of gdpPercap by country, exactly as in the previous exercise, with figsize=(8, 10). In what order do the bars currently appear?
Sort americas_2007 by gdpPercap before plotting, and create the chart again. How does this change what you can read off the chart immediately?
Which country has the highest GDP per capita in the Americas in 2007? Which has the lowest?



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

gapminder = pd.read_csv("data/gapminder.csv")

americas_2007 = gapminder[
    (gapminder["year"] == 2007) & (gapminder["continent"] == "Americas")
]

# 1. Unsorted
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 10))
ax.barh(americas_2007["country"], americas_2007["gdpPercap"])
ax.set_title("Unsorted")
plt.show()









# 2. Sorted
americas_sorted = americas_2007.sort_values("gdpPercap")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 10))
ax.barh(americas_sorted["country"], americas_sorted["gdpPercap"])
ax.set_xlabel("GDP per capita (USD)")
ax.set_ylabel("Country")
ax.set_title("GDP per capita by country, Americas, 2007 (sorted)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








In the unsorted chart, countries appear in whatever order they happened to be listed in the original dataset — typically alphabetical or the order they first appear in the raw data — which has nothing to do with their GDP values. Sorting makes it immediate: the country with the highest GDP per capita appears at one end of the chart, the lowest at the other, and the overall ranking is visible in one glance. In the unsorted version, you would have to scan every single bar and mentally compare lengths to find the extremes — sorting does that comparison for you before the chart is even drawn.




Exercise 4.4.5
This exercise combines data preparation, sorting, and orientation choice using the Titanic dataset.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")

Compute the survival rate for each embarkation port using groupby, and drop any missing values before proceeding.
Sort the result so the highest survival rate appears first.
Decide whether a vertical or horizontal bar chart is more appropriate here, given the number of categories. Create a Figure and Axes with figsize=(7, 5) and plot accordingly.
Add axis labels and a title. Replace the single-letter port codes with full port names in your title or labels for clarity: S = Southampton, C = Cherbourg, Q = Queenstown.



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")

# 1. Survival rate by embarkation port
survival_by_port = titanic.dropna(subset=["embarked"]).groupby("embarked")["survived"].mean()

# 2. Sort by survival rate
survival_by_port = survival_by_port.sort_values(ascending=False)
print(survival_by_port)

embarked
C    0.553571
Q    0.389610
S    0.336957
Name: survived, dtype: float64



# 3-4. Vertical bar chart (only 3 categories, so vertical works fine)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))

ax.bar(survival_by_port.index, survival_by_port.values)

ax.set_xlabel("Embarkation port (S = Southampton, C = Cherbourg, Q = Queenstown)")
ax.set_ylabel("Survival rate")
ax.set_title("Titanic survival rate by embarkation port")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








With only three categories, either orientation works well — vertical bars are a perfectly reasonable default choice here, unlike the 25-country case in the previous exercises where horizontal was necessary. The plot shows that passengers who embarked at Cherbourg had the highest survival rate.

title: Scatter plots
text:
Exercises
We cover scatter plots for visualising the relationship between two numeric variables.

title: Scatter plots
text:
Exercise 4.5.1
Use the healthexp dataset to practice reading a relationship off a basic scatter plot.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv")

Create a Figure and Axes with figsize=(8, 6).
Plot Spending_USD on the x-axis and Life_Expectancy on the y-axis using ax.scatter(). Each point here represents one country in one year.
Add axis labels and a title.
In your own words, describe the relationship shown in the chart. Is it positive, negative, or is there no clear relationship? Does the relationship look like a straight line, or does it flatten out at higher spending levels?



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))

ax.scatter(healthexp["Spending_USD"], healthexp["Life_Expectancy"])

ax.set_xlabel("Health spending per capita (USD)")
ax.set_ylabel("Life expectancy (years)")
ax.set_title("Health spending vs life expectancy")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









There is a positive relationship overall — countries that spend more on health tend to have higher life expectancy. However, the relationship is not a straight line: life expectancy rises quickly at low spending levels but flattens out at higher spending levels, meaning additional spending buys smaller and smaller gains in life expectancy once a country is already spending a lot.





Exercise 4.5.2
Overlapping points can make a scatter plot hard to read. This exercise uses the full titanic dataset, which has enough passengers that points frequently land on top of each other.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")

Create a Figure and Axes with figsize=(8, 6).
Plot age on the x-axis and fare on the y-axis using ax.scatter(), without setting alpha. With 891 passengers, how easy is it to tell where points are most densely packed?
Create the same chart again, this time setting alpha=0.3. How does this change what you can see?
Based on the second chart, where are most passengers concentrated in terms of age and fare?



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")

# 2. Without alpha
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
ax.scatter(titanic["age"], titanic["fare"])
ax.set_title("Without alpha")
plt.show()









# 3. With alpha
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
ax.scatter(titanic["age"], titanic["fare"], alpha=0.3)
ax.set_xlabel("Age")
ax.set_ylabel("Fare")
ax.set_title("Age vs fare (with alpha)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









Without alpha, every point is drawn fully opaque, so in areas where many passengers share similar age and fare values, the points simply stack on top of each other and look identical to a single point — there is no way to tell whether ten passengers or one passenger is represented there.
Setting alpha=0.3 makes each point partially transparent, so overlapping points appear darker than isolated points. Areas with many overlapping passengers now stand out visually as darker patches.
Most passengers are concentrated in the lower-left area of the chart — young to middle-aged adults who paid relatively low fares. The high-fare points are comparatively sparse and spread out, visible now as faint, isolated dots rather than being lost among the dense cluster.





Exercise 4.5.3
The DataFrame below records the valuation and employee count of 15 startup companies. A small number of very large companies dominate the scale.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

startups = pd.DataFrame({
    "company":    [f"Company {i}" for i in range(1, 16)],
    "employees":  [12, 45, 8, 230, 15, 60, 5000, 90, 25, 40,
                   18000, 33, 70, 150, 22],
    "valuation":  [2, 15, 1, 80, 4, 25, 45000, 30, 6, 12,
                   150000, 9, 22, 55, 5]  # millions USD
})

Create a Figure and Axes with figsize=(8, 6).
Plot employees on the x-axis and valuation on the y-axis using ax.scatter(). What does the chart look like? Can you distinguish most of the companies from each other?
Set the x-axis to a log scale using ax.set_xscale("log") and create the chart again, keeping the y-axis as it is. Has this fixed the problem from step 2? Fix the y-axis too, using ax.set_yscale("log").



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

startups = pd.DataFrame({
    "company":    [f"Company {i}" for i in range(1, 16)],
    "employees":  [12, 45, 8, 230, 15, 60, 5000, 90, 25, 40,
                   18000, 33, 70, 150, 22],
    "valuation":  [2, 15, 1, 80, 4, 25, 45000, 30, 6, 12,
                   150000, 9, 22, 55, 5]
})

# 2. Regular scale
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
ax.scatter(startups["employees"], startups["valuation"])
ax.set_title("Regular scale")
plt.show()









# 3. Log scale on both axes
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
ax.scatter(startups["employees"], startups["valuation"])
ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("Employees (log scale)")
ax.set_ylabel("Valuation, millions USD (log scale)")
ax.set_title("Company size vs valuation")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









On a regular scale, two companies with enormous employee counts and valuations squash all 13 other companies into a tiny cluster in the bottom-left corner. It is essentially impossible to distinguish most of the companies from each other or to see any relationship among them.
A log scale helps here because the data spans several orders of magnitude — from 5 employees to 18,000. Log scales are most useful when a variable is extremely right-skewed, with a few very large values dwarfing the rest; for more moderately skewed data, a regular scale is often still perfectly readable.





Exercise 4.5.4
This exercise extends the age-vs-fare scatter plot from exercise 4.5.2 by colouring the points according to passenger class.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")
classes = sorted(titanic["pclass"].unique())

Create a Figure and Axes with figsize=(8, 6).
Use a for loop over classes to plot age vs fare separately for each passenger class, using alpha=0.5 and label= to identify each class.
Add axis labels, a title, and call ax.legend().
Does colouring by class reveal anything about the high-fare outliers visible in exercise 4.5.2?



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")
classes = sorted(titanic["pclass"].unique())

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))

for pclass in classes:
    group = titanic[titanic["pclass"] == pclass]
    ax.scatter(group["age"], group["fare"], alpha=0.5, label=f"Class {pclass}")

ax.set_xlabel("Age")
ax.set_ylabel("Fare")
ax.set_title("Age vs fare by passenger class")
ax.legend()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









Yes — the high-fare outliers are almost entirely first-class passengers (class 1), while second and third class passengers cluster tightly in the lower fare range regardless of age. This makes it clear that fare is driven much more strongly by passenger class than by age, something that was not visible at all in the single-colour version of this chart.





Exercise 4.5.5
This exercise extends the same age-vs-fare relationship once more, this time encoding family size as the size of each point — a bubble chart.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")
titanic["family_size"] = titanic["sibsp"] + titanic["parch"] + 1

family_size counts each passenger plus their siblings/spouses (sibsp) and parents/children (parch) aboard. Print family_size.describe() to see its range.
Create a Figure and Axes with figsize=(9, 6).
Plot age vs fare using ax.scatter(), with alpha=0.4 and marker size set using s=titanic["family_size"] * 20 — multiplying by 20 makes the size differences easier to see.
Add axis labels and a title that mentions what the bubble size represents.



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")
titanic["family_size"] = titanic["sibsp"] + titanic["parch"] + 1

print(titanic["family_size"].describe())

count    891.000000
mean       1.904602
std        1.613459
min        1.000000
25%        1.000000
50%        1.000000
75%        2.000000
max       11.000000
Name: family_size, dtype: float64



fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 6))

ax.scatter(
    titanic["age"], titanic["fare"],
    s=titanic["family_size"] * 20, alpha=0.4
)

ax.set_xlabel("Age")
ax.set_ylabel("Fare")
ax.set_title("Age vs fare, bubble size = family size aboard")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Historgrams
text:
Exercises
We cover histograms for visualising the distribution of a single numeric variable.

title: Historgrams
text:
Exercise 4.6.1
The number of bins you choose has a big effect on what a histogram shows. Use the titanic dataset to see this directly.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")

Create a histogram of fare using ax.hist() with bins=5. What does the distribution look like? Can you tell much about its shape?
Create the same histogram again with bins=30. How does this change what you can see?
Create the same histogram a third time with bins=200. What goes wrong now?
Based on what you observed, what would you say is a reasonable number of bins for this variable, and why?



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")

# 1. Too few bins
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.hist(titanic["fare"], bins=5, edgecolor="white")
ax.set_title("bins=5")
plt.show()









# 2. Reasonable number of bins
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.hist(titanic["fare"], bins=30, edgecolor="white")
ax.set_title("bins=30")
plt.show()









# 3. Too many bins
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.hist(titanic["fare"], bins=200, edgecolor="white")
ax.set_title("bins=200")
plt.show()









With only 5 bins, almost all passengers fall into a single bin at the low end of the fare range. The chart tells you fares are generally low, but nothing more — any finer structure is completely hidden.
With 30 bins, the right-skewed shape becomes clearly visible: a tall concentration of low fares that tapers off into a long tail of higher fares. This is detailed enough to see the shape of the distribution without being too noisy.
With 200 bins, the histogram becomes jagged and full of gaps — many bins contain only one or two passengers, or none at all, since the data is being sliced more finely than there is data to fill it. The overall shape is now harder to see through the noise, not easier.
Somewhere around 20–40 bins works well for this variable and this sample size — fine enough to reveal the skew, coarse enough to stay readable. There is no single correct number; it depends on the sample size and how much detail is useful for the question you are asking.





Exercise 4.6.2
Use the healthexp dataset to practice describing the shape of a distribution.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv")

Create a histogram of Spending_USD with bins=25 and edgecolor="white".
Add axis labels and a title.
In your own words, describe the shape of this distribution. Is it symmetric or skewed? If skewed, in which direction? Does it have one peak or more than one?
Based on the shape you described, would you expect the mean to be higher or lower than the median for this variable?



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))

ax.hist(healthexp["Spending_USD"], bins=25, edgecolor="white")

ax.set_xlabel("Health spending per capita (USD)")
ax.set_ylabel("Count")
ax.set_title("Distribution of health spending")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









The distribution is right-skewed: most country-year observations cluster at lower spending levels, with a long tail stretching out toward a small number of very high-spending observations (largely the USA in recent years). It has a single main peak rather than multiple distinct clusters.
For a right-skewed distribution, the mean is pulled upward by the long tail of high values, so you would expect the mean to be higher than the median.





Exercise 4.6.3
This exercise revisits the mean vs median discussion, this time making the difference visible on a histogram using reference lines.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")

Compute the mean and median of fare.
Create a histogram of fare with bins=30 and edgecolor="white".
Add a vertical reference line at the mean using ax.axvline(), with color="red" and label="Mean". Add a second reference line at the median with color="black" and label="Median".
Add axis labels, a title, and call ax.legend().
Explain, using the chart, why the mean and median differ the way they do.



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")

mean_fare = titanic["fare"].mean()
median_fare = titanic["fare"].median()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))

ax.hist(titanic["fare"], bins=30, edgecolor="white")
ax.axvline(mean_fare, color="red", label="Mean")
ax.axvline(median_fare, color="black", label="Median")

ax.set_xlabel("Fare")
ax.set_ylabel("Count")
ax.set_title("Distribution of fare, with mean and median")
ax.legend()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









The median line sits close to the tall peak of low fares, where most passengers actually paid. The mean line sits further to the right, pulled away from the bulk of the data by the small number of passengers who paid very high fares — visible here as the thin, sparse tail of bars stretching out to the right of the main peak.





Exercise 4.6.4
Comparing two groups’ distributions on the same chart is a common task. Use alpha= to overlay the age distribution of survivors and non-survivors on the Titanic.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")
survivors = titanic[titanic["survived"] == 1]
non_survivors = titanic[titanic["survived"] == 0]

Create a Figure and Axes with figsize=(9, 6).
Plot a histogram of age for survivors with bins=25, alpha=0.5, and label="Survived". Plot a histogram of age for non_survivors on the same Axes with the same bins, alpha=0.5, and label="Did not survive".
Add axis labels, a title, and call ax.legend().
Do the two distributions look noticeably different? Is there any age range where one group appears to be more common than the other?



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")
survivors = titanic[titanic["survived"] == 1]
non_survivors = titanic[titanic["survived"] == 0]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 6))
