Time:                        12:14:03   Log-Likelihood:                -1148.5
No. Observations:                 392   AIC:                             2305.
Df Residuals:                     388   BIC:                             2321.
Df Model:                           3                                         
Covariance Type:            nonrobust                                         
======================================================================================
                         coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Intercept             38.3695      0.781     49.098      0.000      36.833      39.906
C(origin)[T.japan]     2.7510      0.753      3.651      0.000       1.270       4.232
C(origin)[T.usa]      -2.4253      0.678     -3.578      0.000      -3.758      -1.093
horsepower            -0.1336      0.007    -19.474      0.000      -0.147      -0.120
==============================================================================
Omnibus:                       23.486   Durbin-Watson:                   1.036
Prob(Omnibus):                  0.000   Jarque-Bera (JB):               25.987
Skew:                           0.602   Prob(JB):                     2.27e-06
Kurtosis:                       3.376   Cond. No.                         501.
==============================================================================

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.



The reference category is europe, since it does not appear as its own row in the coefficient table — statsmodels drops the first category alphabetically by default, and europe comes before japan and usa.
The coefficient on C(origin)[T.usa] is about -2.4, meaning that American cars have, on average, about 2.4 fewer miles per gallon than European cars at the same horsepower level. The coefficient on C(origin)[T.japan] is positive, meaning Japanese cars average slightly higher fuel efficiency than European cars at the same horsepower level, though this difference is not statistically significant.





Exercise 5.1.4
The scatter plot from exercise 5.1.1 hinted that the relationship between horsepower and fuel efficiency might not be perfectly linear. This exercise tests that directly with a polynomial term.
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg")

Fit a regression of mpg on horsepower and a squared horsepower term, using I(horsepower**2) in the formula. Print the .summary().
Compare the R-squared of this model to the simple linear model from exercise 5.1.1. Does adding the squared term improve the fit?
Is the coefficient on the squared term statistically significant? What does a significant squared term suggest about the shape of the relationship between horsepower and fuel efficiency?



Solution


import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg")

model_poly = smf.ols("mpg ~ horsepower + I(horsepower**2)", data=mpg).fit()
print(model_poly.summary())

                            OLS Regression Results                            
==============================================================================
Dep. Variable:                    mpg   R-squared:                       0.688
Model:                            OLS   Adj. R-squared:                  0.686
Method:                 Least Squares   F-statistic:                     428.0
Date:                Wed, 08 Jul 2026   Prob (F-statistic):           5.40e-99
Time:                        12:14:03   Log-Likelihood:                -1133.2
No. Observations:                 392   AIC:                             2272.
Df Residuals:                     389   BIC:                             2284.
Df Model:                           2                                         
Covariance Type:            nonrobust                                         
======================================================================================
                         coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Intercept             56.9001      1.800     31.604      0.000      53.360      60.440
horsepower            -0.4662      0.031    -14.978      0.000      -0.527      -0.405
I(horsepower ** 2)     0.0012      0.000     10.080      0.000       0.001       0.001
==============================================================================
Omnibus:                       16.158   Durbin-Watson:                   1.078
Prob(Omnibus):                  0.000   Jarque-Bera (JB):               30.662
Skew:                           0.218   Prob(JB):                     2.20e-07
Kurtosis:                       4.299   Cond. No.                     1.29e+05
==============================================================================

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.
[2] The condition number is large, 1.29e+05. This might indicate that there are
strong multicollinearity or other numerical problems.



R-squared rises from about 0.61 in the simple linear model to about 0.69 here, confirming that allowing for curvature improves how well the model fits the data.
The coefficient on the squared term is small in size but highly statistically significant, with a p-value close to zero. A significant, positive squared term alongside a negative linear term means the relationship flattens out at higher horsepower values: fuel efficiency drops sharply as horsepower increases from low levels, but the additional loss in mpg becomes smaller for cars that already have very powerful engines — exactly the curved pattern visible in the original scatter plot.





Exercise 5.1.5
This exercise introduces an interaction term, testing whether the relationship between horsepower and fuel efficiency itself differs by region of origin.
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg")

Fit a regression of mpg on horsepower * C(origin). Print the .summary(). How many coefficients does this model produce, and which ones are new compared to the model in exercise 5.1.3?
Focus on the interaction terms (the rows combining horsepower with an origin category). Are any of them statistically significant?
In plain language, what would a statistically significant interaction term tell you here that the separate horsepower and C(origin) coefficients from exercise 5.1.3 could not?



Solution


import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg")

model_int = smf.ols("mpg ~ horsepower * C(origin)", data=mpg).fit()
print(model_int.summary())

                            OLS Regression Results                            
==============================================================================
Dep. Variable:                    mpg   R-squared:                       0.683
Model:                            OLS   Adj. R-squared:                  0.679
Method:                 Least Squares   F-statistic:                     166.4
Date:                Wed, 08 Jul 2026   Prob (F-statistic):           5.39e-94
Time:                        12:14:03   Log-Likelihood:                -1135.9
No. Observations:                 392   AIC:                             2284.
Df Residuals:                     386   BIC:                             2308.
Df Model:                           5                                         
Covariance Type:            nonrobust                                         
=================================================================================================
                                    coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Intercept                        45.4737      2.225     20.442      0.000      41.100      49.847
C(origin)[T.japan]                3.3425      3.198      1.045      0.297      -2.945       9.630
C(origin)[T.usa]                -10.9972      2.396     -4.589      0.000     -15.708      -6.286
horsepower                       -0.2218      0.027     -8.278      0.000      -0.275      -0.169
horsepower:C(origin)[T.japan]    -0.0082      0.039     -0.211      0.833      -0.085       0.068
horsepower:C(origin)[T.usa]       0.1005      0.028      3.626      0.000       0.046       0.155
==============================================================================
Omnibus:                       27.748   Durbin-Watson:                   1.148
Prob(Omnibus):                  0.000   Jarque-Bera (JB):               31.860
Skew:                           0.644   Prob(JB):                     1.21e-07
Kurtosis:                       3.540   Cond. No.                     2.70e+03
==============================================================================

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.
[2] The condition number is large, 2.7e+03. This might indicate that there are
strong multicollinearity or other numerical problems.



This model has six coefficients: the intercept, the two C(origin) dummy coefficients, the main effect of horsepower, and two new interaction terms — horsepower:C(origin)[T.japan] and horsepower:C(origin)[T.usa] — which were not present in exercise 5.1.3’s model.
The interaction between horsepower and usa is statistically significant, while the interaction between horsepower and japan is not.
A significant interaction term means the slope of the horsepower-mpg relationship itself differs between regions, not just the overall level of fuel efficiency. In exercise 5.1.3, the model assumed all three regions shared the same horsepower penalty and only differed in their starting point (the intercept). Here, the significant interaction for usa indicates that horsepower affects fuel efficiency differently for American cars than for the reference category (Europe) — for example, each additional unit of horsepower might cost American cars less in fuel efficiency than it costs European cars, or vice versa, depending on the sign of the coefficient.

title: Working with model objects
text:
Exercises
We cover how to extract individual components from a fitted model and how to generate predictions and residuals for plotting and diagnostics.

title: Working with model objects
text:
Exercise 5.2.1
Refit the simple regression from exercise 5.1.1, then practice extracting individual pieces of information from the model object instead of reading everything from .summary().
import seaborn as sns
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])

model = smf.ols("mpg ~ horsepower", data=mpg).fit()

Print model.params. What type of object is this?
Print model.bse, model.tvalues, and model.pvalues. What does each of these represent?
Print model.rsquared. How does this compare to reading the R-squared value off .summary()?
Print model.conf_int(). What type of object is this, and what do the two columns represent?
Using only these attributes (not .summary()), write one line of code that prints just the p-value for the horsepower coefficient.



Solution


import seaborn as sns
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])

model = smf.ols("mpg ~ horsepower", data=mpg).fit()

print(model.params)
print(model.bse)
print(model.tvalues)
print(model.pvalues)
print(model.rsquared)
print(model.conf_int())

Intercept     39.935861
horsepower    -0.157845
dtype: float64
Intercept     0.717499
horsepower    0.006446
dtype: float64
Intercept     55.659841
horsepower   -24.489135
dtype: float64
Intercept     1.220362e-187
horsepower     7.031989e-81
dtype: float64
0.6059482578894348
                    0          1
Intercept   38.525212  41.346510
horsepower  -0.170517  -0.145172



# 5. p-value for horsepower only
print(model.pvalues["horsepower"])

7.031989029404482e-81



model.params is a pandas Series, indexed by coefficient name (Intercept and horsepower).
model.bse gives the standard error of each coefficient, model.tvalues gives the t-statistic (the coefficient divided by its standard error), and model.pvalues gives the p-value associated with each t-statistic.
model.rsquared returns the exact same R-squared value shown in .summary() (about 0.606), just as a plain float you can use directly in further calculations, rather than a formatted text block.
model.conf_int() returns a DataFrame with one row per coefficient and two unnamed columns (0 and 1), representing the lower and upper bounds of the 95% confidence interval for each coefficient.





Exercise 5.2.2
Build a tidy results table from the model in the previous exercise, first manually and then using a built-in shortcut.
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])

model = smf.ols("mpg ~ horsepower", data=mpg).fit()

Build a DataFrame called results_table with columns coefficient, std_error, t_stat, p_value, ci_low, and ci_high, using the model attributes from the previous exercise. Round the result to 4 decimal places.
Now use model.summary2().tables[1] to produce essentially the same table in a single line. Compare the column names and values to your manual version.
Given that .summary2() produces this table automatically, when might it still be worth building the table manually as in step 1?



Solution


import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])

model = smf.ols("mpg ~ horsepower", data=mpg).fit()

# 1. Manual tidy table
results_table = pd.DataFrame({
    "coefficient": model.params,
    "std_error":   model.bse,
    "t_stat":      model.tvalues,
    "p_value":     model.pvalues,
    "ci_low":      model.conf_int()[0],
    "ci_high":     model.conf_int()[1],
}).round(4)

print(results_table)

            coefficient  std_error   t_stat  p_value   ci_low  ci_high
Intercept       39.9359     0.7175  55.6598      0.0  38.5252  41.3465
horsepower      -0.1578     0.0064 -24.4891      0.0  -0.1705  -0.1452



# 2. Built-in shortcut
tidy_table = model.summary2().tables[1]
print(tidy_table)

                Coef.  Std.Err.          t          P>|t|     [0.025  \
Intercept   39.935861  0.717499  55.659841  1.220362e-187  38.525212   
horsepower  -0.157845  0.006446 -24.489135   7.031989e-81  -0.170517   

               0.975]  
Intercept   41.346510  
horsepower  -0.145172  



The values are identical — both tables report the same coefficients, standard errors, t-statistics, p-values, and confidence intervals. The only differences are cosmetic: .summary2() uses its own column names (Coef., Std.Err., and so on) and slightly different formatting.
Building the table manually is worth doing when you want specific column names (for example, to match a report template), want to select or reorder only some of the statistics, or want to combine results from several models into one combined table — something .summary2() alone does not do for you.





Exercise 5.2.3
Add in-sample predictions and residuals to the mpg DataFrame, and use them to find which cars the model over- and under-predicts the most.
import seaborn as sns
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"]).reset_index(drop=True)

model = smf.ols("mpg ~ horsepower", data=mpg).fit()

Add two new columns to mpg: fitted, using model.fittedvalues, and residual, using model.resid.
Sort mpg by residual in descending order and print the name, horsepower, mpg, fitted, and residual columns for the top 3 rows. These are the cars whose actual fuel efficiency is highest relative to what the model predicts given their horsepower.
Now sort by residual in ascending order and print the same columns for the bottom 3 rows.
In plain language, what does a large positive residual tell you about a specific car? What might explain why a car outperforms what its horsepoweralone would predict?



Solution


import seaborn as sns
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"]).reset_index(drop=True)

model = smf.ols("mpg ~ horsepower", data=mpg).fit()

# 1. Add fitted values and residuals
mpg["fitted"] = model.fittedvalues
mpg["residual"] = model.resid

# 2. Largest positive residuals
print(mpg.sort_values("residual", ascending=False).head(3)[["name", "horsepower", "mpg", "fitted", "residual"]])

                    name  horsepower   mpg     fitted   residual
320            mazda glc        65.0  46.6  29.675953  16.924047
327  honda civic 1500 gl        67.0  44.6  29.360264  15.239736
330        datsun 280-zx       132.0  32.7  19.100356  13.599644



# 3. Largest negative residuals
print(mpg.sort_values("residual").head(3)[["name", "horsepower", "mpg", "fitted", "residual"]])

                  name  horsepower   mpg     fitted   residual
153      ford maverick        72.0  15.0  28.571040 -13.571040
152    mercury monarch        72.0  15.0  28.571040 -13.571040
198  ford granada ghia        78.0  18.0  27.623972  -9.623972



A large positive residual means the model substantially under-predicts that car’s actual fuel efficiency — the car does much better than its horsepower alone would suggest. The Mazda GLC and Honda Civic, for example, both have modest horsepower but very high actual mpg, well above what the model predicts — likely because they are also unusually lightweight cars, a factor the model does not account for since it only uses horsepower. This is exactly the motivation for the multiple regression in exercise 5.1.2: adding weight as a second predictor should shrink residuals like these.





Exercise 5.2.4
Use out-of-sample prediction to draw the fitted regression line on top of the observed data.
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])

model = smf.ols("mpg ~ horsepower", data=mpg).fit()

Use np.linspace() to generate 100 evenly spaced values of horsepower between its minimum and maximum in the data.
Build a new DataFrame called new_data with a single column named horsepower, containing the values from step 1. This column name must exactly match the variable name used in the model formula.
Use model.predict(new_data) to generate predicted mpg values for these new horsepower values, and add them to new_data as a column called predicted.
Create a scatter plot of the observed data (mpg vs horsepower, with alpha=0.5 and label="Observed"), and overlay the predicted line from new_data in red with label="Regression line". Add axis labels and a legend.



Solution


import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])

model = smf.ols("mpg ~ horsepower", data=mpg).fit()

# 1-3. Build new data and predict
hp_vals = np.linspace(mpg["horsepower"].min(), mpg["horsepower"].max(), 100)
new_data = pd.DataFrame({"horsepower": hp_vals})
new_data["predicted"] = model.predict(new_data)

print(new_data.head())

   horsepower  predicted
0   46.000000  32.675003
1   47.858586  32.381635
2   49.717172  32.088267
3   51.575758  31.794899
4   53.434343  31.501531



# 4. Plot observed data with regression line overlay
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))

ax.scatter(mpg["horsepower"], mpg["mpg"], alpha=0.5, label="Observed")
ax.plot(new_data["horsepower"], new_data["predicted"], color="red", label="Regression line")

ax.set_xlabel("Horsepower")
ax.set_ylabel("Miles per gallon")
ax.set_title("Fuel efficiency vs horsepower, with fitted regression line")
ax.legend()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








The column name in new_data must match horsepower exactly, since model.predict() looks up the formula’s variables by name in whatever DataFrame you pass it — this is the same reason a mismatched or misspelled column name produces a confusing error rather than a silently wrong result.




Exercise 5.2.5
Use a residual plot to check whether the simple linear model from exercise 5.1.1 is missing something, and see whether the quadratic model from exercise 5.1.4 fixes it.
import seaborn as sns
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])

Fit the simple linear model mpg ~ horsepower. Add fitted and residual columns to mpg using .fittedvalues and .resid.
Create a residual plot: scatter fitted (x-axis) against residual (y-axis), with alpha=0.5, and add a horizontal reference line at 0 using ax.axhline(). A well-fitting model should show residuals scattered randomly around zero with no clear pattern. Does this one?
Fit the quadratic model mpg ~ horsepower + I(horsepower**2) from exercise 5.1.4. Add its own fitted_quad and residual_quad columns, and create the same residual plot for this model.
Compare the two residual plots. Does the pattern from step 2 improve in step 3?



Solution


import seaborn as sns
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])

# 1. Simple linear model
model = smf.ols("mpg ~ horsepower", data=mpg).fit()
mpg["fitted"] = model.fittedvalues
mpg["residual"] = model.resid

# 2. Residual plot
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.scatter(mpg["fitted"], mpg["residual"], alpha=0.5)
ax.axhline(0, color="red", linestyle="--")
ax.set_xlabel("Fitted values")
ax.set_ylabel("Residuals")
ax.set_title("Residual plot: linear model")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








The residual plot for the linear model shows a visible curved pattern rather than a random scatter around zero — residuals tend to be positive at both low and high fitted values, and more negative in the middle. This is a classic sign that the model is missing curvature in the true relationship, consistent with what the original scatter plot in exercise 5.1.1 suggested.

# 3. Quadratic model
model_quad = smf.ols("mpg ~ horsepower + I(horsepower**2)", data=mpg).fit()
mpg["fitted_quad"] = model_quad.fittedvalues
mpg["residual_quad"] = model_quad.resid

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))
ax.scatter(mpg["fitted_quad"], mpg["residual_quad"], alpha=0.5)
ax.axhline(0, color="red", linestyle="--")
ax.set_xlabel("Fitted values")
ax.set_ylabel("Residuals")
ax.set_title("Residual plot: quadratic model")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








The residual plot for the quadratic model looks noticeably more like a random scatter around zero, with the curved pattern from the linear model largely gone. This confirms that adding the squared horsepower term captured most of the non-linearity that the simple linear model was missing.

title: Running multiple models
text:
Exercises
We use for loops to automate common regression workflows such as estimating the same model separately across subgroups, and progressively adding variables to build up a set of model specifications.

title: Running multiple models
text:
Exercise 5.3.1
Estimate the relationship between mpg and horsepower separately for each origin group, following the same loop pattern shown in the video.
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])

Get the unique values of origin. Loop over them, and for each one: filter mpg to that group, skip it if it has fewer than 3 rows, fit mpg ~ horsepower, and append a dictionary with the group name, the coefficient on horsepower, and its 95% confidence interval to a list.
Convert the list to a DataFrame called origin_df and print it. Which origin has the steepest (most negative) horsepower penalty?
Now fit a single pooled model on the full mpg data using horsepower * C(origin) — the fully interacted model from exercise 5.1.5. Reconstruct each origin’s slope by adding the relevant interaction coefficient to the main horsepower coefficient (remember, the reference category has no interaction term to add). Compare these reconstructed slopes to the ones in origin_df.
The two approaches should give you the same coefficients. Name one practical difference between running separate regressions per group and running a single pooled model with full interactions.



Solution


import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])

# 1-2. Loop over origin groups
origins = mpg["origin"].unique()
origin_results = []

for origin in origins:
    subset = mpg[mpg["origin"] == origin]
    if len(subset) < 3:
        continue
    fit = smf.ols("mpg ~ horsepower", data=subset).fit()

    origin_results.append({
        "origin": origin,
        "coefficient": fit.params["horsepower"],
        "ci_low": fit.conf_int().loc["horsepower", 0],
        "ci_high": fit.conf_int().loc["horsepower", 1],
    })

origin_df = pd.DataFrame(origin_results)
print(origin_df)

   origin  coefficient    ci_low   ci_high
0     usa    -0.121320 -0.134775 -0.107865
1   japan    -0.230043 -0.287400 -0.172686
2  europe    -0.221835 -0.280688 -0.162982



# 3. Compare to the fully interacted pooled model
model_int = smf.ols("mpg ~ horsepower * C(origin)", data=mpg).fit()
print(model_int.params)

b_hp = model_int.params["horsepower"]
b_hp_japan = model_int.params["horsepower:C(origin)[T.japan]"]
b_hp_usa = model_int.params["horsepower:C(origin)[T.usa]"]

print("Europe slope (interaction model):", b_hp)
print("Japan slope (interaction model):", b_hp + b_hp_japan)
print("USA slope (interaction model):", b_hp + b_hp_usa)

Intercept                        45.473726
C(origin)[T.japan]                3.342488
C(origin)[T.usa]                -10.997230
horsepower                       -0.221835
horsepower:C(origin)[T.japan]    -0.008208
horsepower:C(origin)[T.usa]       0.100515
dtype: float64
Europe slope (interaction model): -0.22183522355296903
Japan slope (interaction model): -0.2300429524548467
USA slope (interaction model): -0.12132003702067533



Japan has the steepest horsepower penalty (about -0.23), followed closely by Europe (about -0.22). American cars have a notably flatter slope (about -0.12).
The reconstructed slopes from the interaction model match the separately estimated slopes in origin_df exactly, since europe is the reference category (no interaction term needed) and the japan/usa interaction terms represent exactly the difference in slope needed to recover each group’s own coefficient.
Both approaches produce identical coefficient estimates when every variable is fully interacted with the group. They differ, however, in how the residual variance is estimated: separate regressions estimate it independently within each group, using only that group’s own observations, while the pooled model assumes one common residual variance across all groups, estimated using the full sample. This means the pooled model’s standard errors (and therefore its confidence intervals) can differ from the separate regressions’ — often tighter, since it borrows statistical strength from the full dataset, which matters especially for smaller groups. The pooled model also lets you test directly whether a slope differs significantly between groups via the interaction term’s p-value, something separate regressions do not give you automatically.





Exercise 5.3.2
Visualize the origin_df results from the previous exercise as a horizontal bar chart with error bars, as shown in the video.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# origin_df from exercise 5.3.1

Create a Figure and Axes, and plot origin_df["coefficient"] against origin_df["origin"] using ax.barh().
Add error bars using ax.errorbar(), with xerr= set to the distance from each coefficient to its lower and upper confidence bound, fmt="none", and capsize=3.
Add axis labels and a title.
Based on the chart, do the confidence intervals for the three origins overlap with each other, or are they clearly separated?



Solution


import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

origin_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "origin": ["usa", "japan", "europe"],
    "coefficient": [-0.121320, -0.230043, -0.221835],
    "ci_low": [-0.134775, -0.287400, -0.280688],
    "ci_high": [-0.107865, -0.172686, -0.162982]
})

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5))

ax.barh(origin_df["origin"], origin_df["coefficient"], color="steelblue")
ax.errorbar(
    origin_df["coefficient"], origin_df["origin"],
    xerr=[origin_df["coefficient"] - origin_df["ci_low"],
          origin_df["ci_high"] - origin_df["coefficient"]],
    fmt="none", color="black", capsize=3
)

ax.set_xlabel("Coefficient on horsepower")
ax.set_title("Effect of horsepower on mpg by origin")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









The confidence interval for the USA is clearly separated from Japan and Europe’s intervals, suggesting American cars genuinely have a flatter horsepower-mpg relationship. Japan and Europe’s intervals overlap substantially with each other, suggesting their slopes are not clearly distinguishable from one another given the sample size within each group.





Exercise 5.3.3
Combine the subgroup loop from 5.3.1 with the regression-line visualization from Topic 5.2: plot each origin’s regression line on the same scatter plot.
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])

Create a Figure and Axes with figsize=(9, 6).
Loop over each origin group. For each one: filter the data, plot the raw points as a scatter with alpha=0.3 (no separate label needed for the points), fit mpg ~ horsepower, generate 50 evenly spaced horsepower values spanning that group’s own range using np.linspace(), predict, and plot the resulting line with label=origin.
Add axis labels, a title, and call ax.legend().
Do the three lines look roughly parallel, or do they clearly diverge? Does this match what you found in exercise 5.3.1?



Solution


import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 6))

for origin in mpg["origin"].unique():
    subset = mpg[mpg["origin"] == origin]
    fit = smf.ols("mpg ~ horsepower", data=subset).fit()

    hp_vals = np.linspace(subset["horsepower"].min(), subset["horsepower"].max(), 50)
    new_data = pd.DataFrame({"horsepower": hp_vals})
    new_data["predicted"] = fit.predict(new_data)

    ax.scatter(subset["horsepower"], subset["mpg"], alpha=0.3)
    ax.plot(new_data["horsepower"], new_data["predicted"], label=origin)

ax.set_xlabel("Horsepower")
ax.set_ylabel("Miles per gallon")
ax.set_title("Fuel efficiency vs horsepower by origin, with fitted lines")
ax.legend()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









The American line is visibly flatter than the Japanese and European lines, which slope down more steeply and sit close to parallel with each other — the same pattern already visible numerically in the coefficients from exercise 5.3.1. Building the chart this way makes that difference immediately visible, rather than requiring you to compare three separate numbers by eye.





Exercise 5.3.4
Practice the progressive-variable loop pattern from the video, this time using mpg and its own set of explanatory variables.
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])
mpg["weight_tons"] = mpg["weight"] / 1000

Define a list controls = ["horsepower", "weight_tons", "C(origin)"].
Loop over range(1, len(controls) + 1). On each iteration, use " + ".join() to build the right-hand side of the formula from controls[:i], construct the full formula string with an f-string, print it, fit the model, and append the fitted model to a list called models.
Print the .summary() of the last model in the list — the one with all three variables.



Solution


import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])
mpg["weight_tons"] = mpg["weight"] / 1000

controls = ["horsepower", "weight_tons", "C(origin)"]
models = []

for i in range(1, len(controls) + 1):
    rhs = " + ".join(controls[:i])
    formula = f"mpg ~ {rhs}"
    print(f"Model {i}: {formula}")
    models.append(smf.ols(formula, data=mpg).fit())

Model 1: mpg ~ horsepower
Model 2: mpg ~ horsepower + weight_tons
Model 3: mpg ~ horsepower + weight_tons + C(origin)



print(models[-1].summary())

                            OLS Regression Results                            
==============================================================================

Model:                            OLS   Adj. R-squared:                  0.716
Method:                 Least Squares   F-statistic:                     247.9
Date:                Mon, 13 Jul 2026   Prob (F-statistic):          2.44e-105
Time:                        13:14:24   Log-Likelihood:                -1112.2
No. Observations:                 392   AIC:                             2234.
Df Residuals:                     387   BIC:                             2254.
Df Model:                           4                                         
Covariance Type:            nonrobust                                         
======================================================================================
                         coef    std err          t      P>|t|      [0.025      0.975]
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Intercept             43.7011      0.932     46.871      0.000      41.868      45.534
C(origin)[T.japan]     1.7811      0.696      2.558      0.011       0.412       3.150
C(origin)[T.usa]      -0.9611      0.640     -1.501      0.134      -2.220       0.298
horsepower            -0.0535      0.011     -4.875      0.000      -0.075      -0.032
weight_tons           -4.8427      0.545     -8.878      0.000      -5.915      -3.770
==============================================================================
Omnibus:                       35.026   Durbin-Watson:                   0.914
Prob(Omnibus):                  0.000   Jarque-Bera (JB):               47.308
Skew:                           0.658   Prob(JB):                     5.33e-11
Kurtosis:                       4.078   Cond. No.                         576.
==============================================================================

Notes:
[1] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.


The loop builds three increasingly complex formulas — mpg ~ horsepower, mpg ~ horsepower + weight_tons, and mpg ~ horsepower + weight_tons + C(origin) — using exactly the same .join() and slicing pattern as the video, just applied to a different list of controls.




Exercise 5.3.5
Use the models list from the previous exercise to see how model fit changes as variables are added.
# models list from exercise 5.3.4

Loop over models with enumerate(), starting the count at 1, and print each model’s number alongside its R-squared, rounded to 4 decimal places.
By how much does R-squared increase when weight_tons is added to the model with just horsepower? By how much does it increase again when C(origin) is added on top of that?
Based on these increases, which variable contributes more additional explanatory power: weight_tons or C(origin)?



Solution


for i, model in enumerate(models, start=1):
    print(f"Model {i} R-squared: {model.rsquared:.4f}")

Model 1 R-squared: 0.6059
Model 2 R-squared: 0.7064
Model 3 R-squared: 0.7193



R-squared rises from about 0.6059 to about 0.7064 when weight_tons is added — an increase of roughly 0.10. Adding C(origin) on top brings R-squared to about 0.7193 — a much smaller further increase of roughly 0.013.
weight_tons contributes far more additional explanatory power than C(origin). This is a useful pattern to notice: the biggest jumps in R-squared often come from the first one or two genuinely important variables, while additional variables — even meaningful ones — tend to add progressively less once the main drivers are already in the model.

title: Exporting tables and results
text:
Exercises
We cover how to get your results out of Python and into a report: saving DataFrames to Excel, and building publication-style regression tables with the stargazer package.

title: Exporting tables and results
text:
Exercise 5.4.1
Build a summary table on the mpg dataset and export it to Excel.
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])

Group mpg by origin and compute the number of cars, the mean mpg, and the mean horsepower for each group, using named aggregations. Round the result to 1 decimal place and reset the index.
Save the result to an Excel file.



Solution


import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])

summary = (
    mpg.groupby("origin")
    .agg(
        n_cars=("name", "count"),
        mean_mpg=("mpg", "mean"),
        mean_hp=("horsepower", "mean")
    )
    .round(1)
    .reset_index()
)

print(summary)

# Uncomment the line below to save the file — make sure an "output" folder
# already exists in your project directory first, or this will raise an error.
# summary.to_excel("output/origin_summary.xlsx", index=False)

   origin  n_cars  mean_mpg  mean_hp
0  europe      68      27.6     80.6
1   japan      79      30.5     79.8
2     usa     245      20.0    119.0


American cars have the lowest average fuel efficiency and the highest average horsepower among the three origins, consistent with the patterns explored throughout this topic’s regression exercises.




Exercise 5.4.2
Use the models list from exercise 5.3.4 to build a formatted regression table with Stargazer.
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
from stargazer.stargazer import Stargazer

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])
mpg["weight_tons"] = mpg["weight"] / 1000

controls = ["horsepower", "weight_tons", "C(origin)"]
models = []
for i in range(1, len(controls) + 1):
    rhs = " + ".join(controls[:i])
    formula = f"mpg ~ {rhs}"
    models.append(smf.ols(formula, data=mpg).fit())

Pass models to Stargazer() to build a side-by-side comparison table.
Display the table (simply write table on its own line, or use print() in a script). Look at the variable names in the leftmost column — are they easy to read, or do they still look like raw statsmodels output?



Solution


import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
from stargazer.stargazer import Stargazer

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])
mpg["weight_tons"] = mpg["weight"] / 1000

controls = ["horsepower", "weight_tons", "C(origin)"]
models = []
for i in range(1, len(controls) + 1):
    rhs = " + ".join(controls[:i])
    formula = f"mpg ~ {rhs}"
    models.append(smf.ols(formula, data=mpg).fit())

table = Stargazer(models)
table


Dependent variable: mpg(1)(2)(3)


C(origin)[T.japan]1.781**
(0.696)
C(origin)[T.usa]-0.961
(0.640)
Intercept39.936***45.640***43.701***
(0.717)(0.793)(0.932)
horsepower-0.158***-0.047***-0.054***
(0.006)(0.011)(0.011)
weight_tons-5.794***-4.843***
(0.502)(0.545)


Observations392392392R20.6060.7060.719Adjusted R20.6050.7050.716Residual Std. Error4.906 (df=390)4.240 (df=389)4.157 (df=387)F Statistic599.718*** (df=1; 390)467.910*** (df=2; 389)247.899*** (df=4; 387)
Note:*p<0.1; **p<0.05; ***p<0.01



The table already shows all three models side by side with stars marking statistical significance, which is a big improvement over reading three separate .summary() outputs. However, the variable names — weight_tons, C(origin)[T.japan], and so on — are still the raw internal statsmodels names, not something you would want to show in a report.





Exercise 5.4.3
Customize the table from the previous exercise so it is ready to include in a report.

Use rename_covariates() to relabel horsepower, weight_tons, C(origin)[T.japan], C(origin)[T.usa], and Intercept with reader-friendly names of your choice.
Use covariate_order() to list the covariates in the order you want them to appear — for example, horsepower and weight_tons first, then the origin dummies, with Intercept last.
Add a title with .title(), set the dependent variable’s display name with .dependent_variable_name(), and add a note with .add_custom_notes().



Solution


table.rename_covariates({
    "horsepower":            "Horsepower",
    "weight_tons":           "Weight (tons)",
    "C(origin)[T.japan]":    "Japan",
    "C(origin)[T.usa]":      "USA",
    "Intercept":             "Intercept",
})

table.covariate_order([
    "horsepower",
    "weight_tons",
    "C(origin)[T.japan]",
    "C(origin)[T.usa]",
    "Intercept",
])

table.title("OLS Regression: Fuel Efficiency")
table.dependent_variable_name("Miles per Gallon")
table.add_custom_notes(["Standard errors in parentheses."])

table

OLS Regression: Fuel Efficiency
Dependent variable: Miles per Gallon(1)(2)(3)


Horsepower-0.158***-0.047***-0.054***
(0.006)(0.011)(0.011)
Weight (tons)-5.794***-4.843***
(0.502)(0.545)
Japan1.781**
(0.696)
USA-0.961
(0.640)
Intercept39.936***45.640***43.701***
(0.717)(0.793)(0.932)


Observations392392392R20.6060.7060.719Adjusted R20.6050.7050.716Residual Std. Error4.906 (df=390)4.240 (df=389)4.157 (df=387)F Statistic599.718*** (df=1; 390)467.910*** (df=2; 389)247.899*** (df=4; 387)
Note:*p<0.1; **p<0.05; ***p<0.01Standard errors in parentheses.


The table now reads like something you could put directly into a report: clear variable names in a sensible order, a descriptive title, and a note explaining what is shown in parentheses. This is the same set of customization steps shown in the video, applied to the mpg models instead of the gapminder ones.




Exercise 5.4.4
Save the customized table to both HTML and LaTeX, using the with open(...) pattern from the video.

Save the table as an HTML file at output/mpg_regression_table.html using table.render_html() inside a with open(...) as f: block.
Save the same table as a LaTeX file at output/mpg_regression_table.tex using table.render_latex(), following the same pattern.
If you have an output folder in your project, open the HTML file in a browser. Does it display the table correctly?



Solution


# Uncomment the two blocks below to save the files — make sure an "output"
# folder already exists in your project directory first, or this will raise
# an error.

# with open("output/mpg_regression_table.html", "w") as f:
#     f.write(table.render_html())

# with open("output/mpg_regression_table.tex", "w") as f:
#     f.write(table.render_latex())

print("Both render_html() and render_latex() produce a string, which the")
print("with open(...) block writes directly to a file.")

Both render_html() and render_latex() produce a string, which the
with open(...) block writes directly to a file.


The HTML version opens in a browser like a normal table and can be copy-pasted into Word. The LaTeX version contains a tabular environment that can be \input{} directly into a LaTeX document — you would not open it directly and expect it to look like a table, since LaTeX source only renders after being compiled.




Exercise 5.4.5
The video told you not to worry about exactly how the with keyword works — just to treat with open(...) as f: as a standard pattern. This exercise shows you a second, more familiar situation where the exact same pattern shows up: applying a style sheet to a single chart.
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.linspace(0, 10, 50)
y = np.sin(x)
Recall from Topic 4.7 that plt.style.use("ggplot") changes the style for every chart created afterward in the same session, until you explicitly reset it with plt.style.use("default").

Instead, use with plt.style.context("ggplot"): and create a Figure and Axes plotting x against y inside the with block.
After the with block ends, create a second, completely separate Figure and Axes plotting the same data. Does this second chart use the ggplot style, or has it reverted to the default?
Both with open(...) as f: and with plt.style.context(...): follow the same shape: something is temporarily set up, your code runs inside the block, and then it is automatically cleaned up again when the block ends — closing the file in one case, reverting the style in the other. Why is this pattern more convenient here than calling plt.style.use() and remembering to reset it manually afterward?



Solution


import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.linspace(0, 10, 50)
y = np.sin(x)

# 1. Style applied only inside the with block
with plt.style.context("ggplot"):
    fig, ax = plt.subplots()
    ax.plot(x, y)
    ax.set_title("Styled chart (ggplot)")
    plt.show()









# 2. Second chart, outside the with block
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(x, y)
ax.set_title("Unstyled chart (default)")
plt.show()









The second chart uses the default matplotlib style, not ggplot. The style change from plt.style.context() only applies to code running inside the with block — as soon as the block ends, matplotlib automatically reverts to whatever style was active before.
With plt.style.use(), you have to remember to call plt.style.use("default") afterward yourself — if you forget, every subsequent chart in your script keeps the style, which can be a confusing bug if you only wanted it for one specific chart. The with pattern removes this risk entirely: the cleanup happens automatically the moment the indented block ends, whether that block finishes normally or even if an error occurs partway through. This is exactly the same benefit with open(...) gives you for files — you never have to remember to call f.close() yourself.

title: Reproducible reporting with Jupyter notebooks
text:
Exercises
We introduce Jupyter notebooks as a way to combine code, output, and written explanation in a single document, and walk through a prepared notebook that puts together several skills from this course into a small analysis.
The notebook used in the video is available on GitHub: skl401-notebook.ipynb. Open the page and use the download button there to save it to your computer.

title: Reproducible reporting with Jupyter notebooks
text:
Exercise 5.5.1
Start a new Jupyter notebook. You will build it up progressively across the exercises on this page, ending with a small finished analysis.

Add a markdown cell at the top with a title (# Health Spending and Life Expectancy) and one sentence describing what the notebook explores: how health spending and life expectancy compare across six countries, using the healthexp dataset.
Add a code cell below it that imports pandas, seaborn, and matplotlib.pyplot, loads healthexp with sns.load_dataset(), and prints its shape.
Run both cells. Confirm the shape appears directly below the code cell, and that the markdown cell renders as formatted text rather than plain code.




Exercise 5.5.2
Continue the same notebook by adding a summary of the data.

Add a markdown cell with a short header introducing a question: how does health spending compare across countries?
Add a code cell below it that groups healthexp by Country, computes the mean Spending_USD and mean Life_Expectancy for each, rounds to 1 decimal place, resets the index, and sorts by mean spending in descending order. Assign the result to summary.
Run the cell and confirm the table appears as output directly below the code.




Exercise 5.5.3
Add a “dynamic sentence” to the notebook — a sentence that inserts an actual computed value from your data, rather than a number you typed in by hand.

Add a code cell that extracts the country name and mean spending value from the first row of summary (the highest-spending country).
In the same cell, use an f-string inside a print() statement to display a sentence such as: "The country with the highest average health spending is USA, at $4388.6 per capita." — but built from the variables you just extracted, not typed as a fixed string.




Exercise 5.5.4
Add a chart to visualize the comparison from exercise 5.5.2.

Add a markdown cell with a short line introducing the chart: a bar chart will make the differences across countries easier to see than the table alone.
Add a code cell that creates a horizontal bar chart of summary, with country on the y-axis and mean spending on the x-axis, using figsize=(8, 5) and a colour of your choice. Add an axis label and a title.
Run the cell and confirm the chart appears as output directly below the code, in the same way the table did in exercise 5.5.2.




Exercise 5.5.5
Export the finished notebook to a format you could share with someone who does not have Python installed.

In Positron, find the notebook export option in the notebook toolbar and export your notebook to HTML. (If you’d rather do this from a terminal: it requires a package called nbconvert, which isn’t installed by default in this course, so run pip install nbconvert once, then python -m nbconvert --to html your-notebook.ipynb — on a Mac, use python3 instead of python.)
Open the resulting HTML file in a browser. Does it look the same as the notebook?
Try clicking on one of the code cells in the exported HTML file. Can you edit it and re-run it? What does this tell you about the difference between the notebook file itself and an exported copy of it?


A complete, worked version of this notebook — with all five exercises finished and every cell executed — is available on GitHub: 05-05-exercise-solution.ipynb. Open the page and use the download button there to save it to your computer.

title: Debugging: reading error messages
text:
Exercises
We cover how to read a Python traceback, work through the most common error types you will encounter, and cover the difference between an error and a warning.

title: Debugging: reading error messages
text:
Exercise 5.6.1
Each of the two code snippets below raises an error. For each one: run it, identify the error type from the traceback, explain in plain language what went wrong, and then fix it.
import seaborn as sns

healthexp = sns.load_dataset("healthexp")

# Snippet A
print(healthex["Country"].unique())
# Snippet B
print(healthexp["country"].unique())


Solution


import seaborn as sns

healthexp = sns.load_dataset("healthexp")

# Snippet A — NameError, fixed
print(healthexp["Country"].unique())

<StringArray>
['Germany', 'France', 'Great Britain', 'Japan', 'USA', 'Canada']
Length: 6, dtype: str



# Snippet B — KeyError, fixed
print(healthexp["Country"].unique())

<StringArray>
['Germany', 'France', 'Great Britain', 'Japan', 'USA', 'Canada']
Length: 6, dtype: str


Snippet A raises a NameError: name 'healthex' is not defined. The variable was misspelled — healthex instead of healthexp. This is the most common error beginners encounter, and the fix is almost always a typo in a variable name.
Snippet B raises a KeyError: 'country'. The DataFrame does not have a column called country — the actual column is Country, capitalised. KeyError almost always means a column name is misspelled or has the wrong case; check .columns if you are not sure of the exact spelling.




Exercise 5.6.2
Each of the two code snippets below raises an error. For each one: run it, identify the error type, explain what went wrong, and fix it.
import seaborn as sns

titanic = sns.load_dataset("titanic")

# Snippet A
titanic["class"] = titanic["class"].strip()
# Snippet B
print(titanic.iloc[1000])


Solution


import seaborn as sns

titanic = sns.load_dataset("titanic")

# Snippet A — AttributeError, fixed
titanic["class"] = titanic["class"].str.strip()


# Snippet B — IndexError, fixed
print(titanic.iloc[titanic.shape[0] - 1])

survived                0
pclass                  3
sex                  male
age                  32.0
sibsp                   0
parch                   0
fare                 7.75
embarked                Q
class               Third
who                   man
adult_male           True
deck                  NaN
embark_town    Queenstown
alive                  no
alone                True
Name: 890, dtype: object


Snippet A raises an AttributeError: 'Series' object has no attribute 'strip'. .strip() is a plain Python string method — it works on a single string, not on an entire column at once. To apply a string method to every value in a column, you need the .str accessor: .str.strip(). Forgetting .str is one of the most common AttributeErrors in pandas work.
Snippet B raises an IndexError: single positional indexer is out-of-bounds. The Titanic dataset only has 891 rows (positions 0–890), so position 1000 does not exist. IndexError means you have asked for a position beyond the end (or before the start) of your data — checking .shape first tells you the valid range.




Exercise 5.6.3
Each of the two code snippets below raises an error. For each one: run it, identify the error type, explain what went wrong, and fix it.
import seaborn as sns

titanic = sns.load_dataset("titanic")

# Snippet A
result = "Mean age: " + titanic["age"].mean()
# Snippet B
mean_age = titanic["age"].mean()

if mean_age > 25
    print("High average age")


Solution


import seaborn as sns

titanic = sns.load_dataset("titanic")

# Snippet A — TypeError, fixed
result = f"Mean age: {titanic['age'].mean():.1f}"
print(result)

Mean age: 29.7



# Snippet B — SyntaxError, fixed
mean_age = titanic["age"].mean()

if mean_age > 25:
    print("High average age")

High average age


Snippet A raises a TypeError: can only concatenate str (not "numpy.float64") to str. The + operator cannot combine a string and a number directly. The fix is to convert the number to a string first (e.g. str(...)), or — usually cleaner — use an f-string.
Snippet B raises a SyntaxError: expected ':'. Every if statement in Python needs a colon at the end of the condition. Python points a caret (^) at roughly where it got confused, which is often right where the missing colon should be.




Exercise 5.6.4
Each of the two code snippets below raises an error. For each one: run it, identify the error type, explain what went wrong, and fix it.
import pandas as pd

# Snippet A
df = pd.read_csv("files/titanic_data.csv")
# Snippet B
import stargazr


Solution


import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns

# Snippet A — FileNotFoundError, fixed
titanic = sns.load_dataset("titanic")      # import from seaborn
titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")  # or import from data subfolder
print(titanic.shape)

(891, 15)



# Snippet B — ModuleNotFoundError, fixed (spelling corrected)
import stargazer

Snippet A raises a FileNotFoundError: No such file or directory: 'files/titanic_data.csv'. Either the file does not exist at that path, the filename is wrong, or the folder structure is different from what you expect — for example, data/titanic.csv rather than files/titanic_data.csv. This connects directly to the relative-vs-absolute path lesson from Topic 3.1: the path is always relative to your current working directory, so double-check both the filename and the folder.
Snippet B raises a ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'stargazr'. The package name is misspelled — it should be stargazer. If the spelling is correct and you still get this error, it usually means the package has not been installed yet, and you need to run pip install <package-name> first.




Exercise 5.6.5
Unlike an error, a warning does not stop your code from running — but it is still worth understanding. Run the code below.
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])
mpg.loc[0, "horsepower"] = 0
mpg.loc[1, "horsepower"] = -5

mpg["log_horsepower"] = np.log(mpg["horsepower"])

What warning appears? Does the code still finish running despite it?
Print mpg["log_horsepower"].head(). What values appear in the first two rows, and why?
In your own words, what does this RuntimeWarning tell you about the underlying data, and what would you check or fix before trusting the rest of this column?



Solution


import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])
mpg.loc[0, "horsepower"] = 0
mpg.loc[1, "horsepower"] = -5

mpg["log_horsepower"] = np.log(mpg["horsepower"])

print(mpg["log_horsepower"].head())

0        -inf
1         NaN
2    5.010635
3    5.010635
4    4.941642
Name: log_horsepower, dtype: float64


C:\Users\s14754\AppData\Local\Python\pythoncore-3.14-64\Lib\site-packages\pandas\core\arraylike.py:402: RuntimeWarning: divide by zero encountered in log
  result = getattr(ufunc, method)(*inputs, **kwargs)
C:\Users\s14754\AppData\Local\Python\pythoncore-3.14-64\Lib\site-packages\pandas\core\arraylike.py:402: RuntimeWarning: invalid value encountered in log
  result = getattr(ufunc, method)(*inputs, **kwargs)



A RuntimeWarning appears — divide by zero encountered in log and invalid value encountered in log. The code still finishes running; the warning does not stop execution the way an error would.
The first row is -inf (the log of 0, which is undefined and represented as negative infinity), and the second row is NaN (the log of a negative number, which has no real value).
A RuntimeWarning from a numerical operation like np.log() almost always means the input contains values the operation cannot handle — here, zero or negative horsepower, which should not exist for a real car. Before trusting this column further, you would want to go back and check why the original horsepower column contains zero or negative values in the first place — whether it is a genuine data entry error, a placeholder for missing data, or (as in this exercise) values you introduced yourself — and either correct, filter, or otherwise handle those rows before using the log-transformed column in any analysis.

title: Reading documentation and getting help
text:
Exercises
We cover how to read function documentation, and how to search effectively when documentation alone does not solve your problem.

title: Reading documentation and getting help
text:
Exercise 5.7.1
Import store_sales.csv from the URL and try to load it with a plain pd.read_csv() call.
import pandas as pd

url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/data/store_sales.csv"
df = pd.read_csv(url)
print(df)
The result is clearly wrong — everything has landed in one or two columns.

Open the raw file in a text editor (or look at the printed output above) and identify what is different about this file compared to a standard CSV. How many metadata lines sit above the actual header row? What character separates the values?
Look up the documentation for pd.read_csv() — either online or with help(pd.read_csv) — and find the parameter that skips a fixed number of rows at the top of a file. Use it together with the correct separator to load the file so the columns appear correctly.
One column contains a code, NA_CODE, that should be treated as a missing value rather than as text. Find the relevant parameter in the documentation’s Examples section and use it to fix this.
The internal_flag column is not useful for analysis. Use usecols= to load only date, store_id, region, units_sold, and revenue.
Print the dtypes of your final result. Do date and store_id look like you would expect a date and an ID column to look?



Solution


import pandas as pd

url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/data/store_sales.csv"

df = pd.read_csv(
    url,
    sep=";",
    skiprows=2,
    na_values=["NA_CODE"],
    usecols=["date", "store_id", "region", "units_sold", "revenue"]
)

print(df)
print(df.dtypes)

         date  store_id region  units_sold  revenue
0  2023-01-05         7  North         120   3600.0
1  2023-01-06        12  South          95      NaN
2  2023-01-07         7  North         110   3300.0
3  2023-01-08         4   East          99   2970.0
4  2023-01-09        12  South         130      NaN
date              str
store_id        int64
region            str
units_sold      int64
revenue       float64
dtype: object


Task 1: There are two metadata lines above the real header row, and values are separated by semicolons rather than commas.
Task 3: Without na_values=["NA_CODE"], the revenue column would be read as text (an object column) rather than as numbers, since pandas has no way of knowing "NA_CODE" is meant to represent a missing value rather than a genuine text entry.
Task 5: date is still read as plain text (object), not a real date, and store_id has silently lost its leading zeros — 007 became the integer 7. Both are genuine problems, and neither sep, skiprows, na_values, nor usecols fixes them. This is exactly what the next exercise addresses.




Exercise 5.7.2
Continue with store_sales.csv, using the working combination of parameters from the previous exercise. This exercise introduces two read_csv parameters that were not covered in the video.

store_id lost its leading zeros during import — 007 became 7. Look up the dtype= parameter in the documentation and use it to force store_id to be read as text instead, preserving the leading zeros.
date is still stored as plain text rather than an actual date. Look up the parse_dates= parameter and use it to convert date to a proper datetime column at the moment of import, rather than converting it afterward with pd.to_datetime().
Print the dtypes of the final result to confirm both columns now look correct.



Solution


import pandas as pd

url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/data/store_sales.csv"

df = pd.read_csv(
    url,
    sep=";",
    skiprows=2,
    na_values=["NA_CODE"],
    usecols=["date", "store_id", "region", "units_sold", "revenue"],
    dtype={"store_id": str},
    parse_dates=["date"]
)

print(df)
print(df.dtypes)

        date store_id region  units_sold  revenue
0 2023-01-05      007  North         120   3600.0
1 2023-01-06      012  South          95      NaN
2 2023-01-07      007  North         110   3300.0
3 2023-01-08      004   East          99   2970.0
4 2023-01-09      012  South         130      NaN
date          datetime64[us]
store_id                 str
region                   str
units_sold             int64
revenue              float64
dtype: object



Passing dtype={"store_id": str} tells pandas to read that specific column as text from the start, before it has a chance to interpret 007 as the number 7 and drop the leading zeros. This matters any time an ID column happens to look numeric — postal codes and phone numbers have the exact same problem.
parse_dates=["date"] converts the column to a proper datetime during the read itself, producing the same result as calling pd.to_datetime() afterward, but in one step at import time rather than two.





Exercise 5.7.3
This exercise practices reading documentation for a function you have not used anywhere in this course: pd.cut(), which bins a continuous numeric column into discrete categories.
import seaborn as sns

titanic = sns.load_dataset("titanic").dropna(subset=["age"])

Look up the documentation for pd.cut() — either online or with help(pd.cut). Based on the description and the parameter list, what are the two main arguments you need to provide, and what does each one do?
Use pd.cut() to create an age_group column with three categories: "child" (0–18), "adult" (18–60), and "senior" (60–100). You will need to pass a list of bin edges and a list of labels.
Print .value_counts() on the result. Back in the Topic 3, you built an equivalent age classification using np.select() with manually written conditions. Which approach do you find more concise for this specific task?



Solution


import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd

titanic = sns.load_dataset("titanic").dropna(subset=["age"])

bins = [0, 18, 60, 100]
labels = ["child", "adult", "senior"]
titanic["age_group"] = pd.cut(titanic["age"], bins=bins, labels=labels)

print(titanic["age_group"].value_counts())

age_group
adult     553
child     139
senior     22
Name: count, dtype: int64


Task 1: pd.cut() needs a list of bin edges (bins=) defining the boundaries between categories, and a matching list of category names (labels=) one shorter than the number of edges, since each pair of adjacent edges defines one bin.
Task 3: pd.cut() is considerably more concise here than np.select() — a single line replaces the conditions and choices lists needed before. This is another example of the recurring lesson from Topic 3: before writing conditional logic by hand, it is worth checking whether pandas already has a dedicated function for exactly the task you need.




Exercise 5.7.4
This exercise practices turning a vague search query into an effective one. For each bad query below, a situation is described. Rewrite the query so it would actually lead to a useful, specific answer, and explain in one sentence what you changed and why.

Situation: You wrote titanic["class"] = titanic["class"].strip() and got an AttributeError. Bad query: python string method not working
Situation: You have two DataFrames that share a column, and you want to combine them into one, but you cannot remember the function name. Bad query: python combine two tables
Situation: You plotted a date column against some values with matplotlib, but the x-axis labels are overlapping and unreadable. Bad query: python plot date axis looks wrong



Solution

Example answers — yours do not need to match exactly, but should follow the same reasoning.

Good query: pandas AttributeError str strip Series. The bad query never mentions pandas, the specific method (strip), the object involved (a Series), or the actual error type — all of which narrow the search directly to this specific mistake rather than any generic string-processing issue in any context.
Good query: pandas merge two dataframes on column. Naming the package (pandas) and describing the task using the same words the documentation itself uses — “merge,” “dataframes,” “column” — leads directly to pd.merge(), even without knowing the function name in advance.
Good query: matplotlib date axis labels overlapping. The bad query only adds the vague word “wrong,” which tells a search engine nothing specific. Naming the library (matplotlib), the part of the chart involved (the date axis), and the precise, observable symptom (labels overlapping) gives a search engine enough to work with — without requiring you to already know that the underlying cause is a string column masquerading as a date, which is exactly the thing you are trying to discover.

A good rule of thumb across all three: always include the package name, and either the function name, the exact error type, or the specific mechanism involved. Vague verbs like “not working” or “looks wrong” carry almost no useful information for a search engine.




Exercise 5.7.5
This exercise introduces three Stargazer methods not shown in the video, using the models list built in exercise 5.3.4.
import seaborn as sns
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
from stargazer.stargazer import Stargazer

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])
mpg["weight_tons"] = mpg["weight"] / 1000

controls = ["horsepower", "weight_tons", "C(origin)"]
models = []
for i in range(1, len(controls) + 1):
    rhs = " + ".join(controls[:i])
    models.append(smf.ols(f"mpg ~ {rhs}", data=mpg).fit())

table = Stargazer(models)
Take a look at the Github page for Stargazer (and especially the example notebook) to explore the available methods in the package.

Use significant_digits() to display 2 decimal places instead of the default.
Use show_confidence_intervals(True) to display confidence intervals instead of standard errors.
Use custom_columns() to replace the default (1), (2), (3) column headers with more descriptive labels of your choice — for example "Basic", "Plus weight", and "Plus origin".
Display the resulting table. How does it compare to the version you built in exercise 5.4.3?



Solution


import seaborn as sns
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
from stargazer.stargazer import Stargazer

mpg = sns.load_dataset("mpg").dropna(subset=["horsepower"])
mpg["weight_tons"] = mpg["weight"] / 1000

controls = ["horsepower", "weight_tons", "C(origin)"]
models = []
for i in range(1, len(controls) + 1):
    rhs = " + ".join(controls[:i])
    models.append(smf.ols(f"mpg ~ {rhs}", data=mpg).fit())

table = Stargazer(models)

table.significant_digits(2)
table.show_confidence_intervals(True)
table.custom_columns(["Basic", "Plus weight", "Plus origin"], [1, 1, 1])

table


Dependent variable: mpgBasicPlus weightPlus origin(1)(2)(3)


C(origin)[T.japan]1.78**
(0.41 , 3.15)
C(origin)[T.usa]-0.96
(-2.22 , 0.30)
Intercept39.94***45.64***43.70***
(38.53 , 41.35)(44.08 , 47.20)(41.87 , 45.53)
horsepower-0.16***-0.05***-0.05***
(-0.17 , -0.15)(-0.07 , -0.03)(-0.08 , -0.03)
weight_tons-5.79***-4.84***
(-6.78 , -4.81)(-5.92 , -3.77)


Observations392392392R20.610.710.72Adjusted R20.600.700.72Residual Std. Error4.91 (df=390)4.24 (df=389)4.16 (df=387)F Statistic599.72*** (df=1; 390)467.91*** (df=2; 389)247.90*** (df=4; 387)
Note:*p<0.1; **p<0.05; ***p<0.01



The table now shows confidence intervals instead of standard errors beneath each coefficient, two decimal places throughout, and descriptive column headers instead of plain model numbers — all genuinely useful options that were never mentioned in the video. Finding these required looking past the five methods actually demonstrated and scanning the full list of available methods yourself, which is exactly the documentation-reading skill this video is about.

title: Using generative AI
text:
Exercises
We cover how to use generative AI responsibly: where it genuinely helps, where it commonly goes wrong, and some practical guidelines for checking its output.

title: Using generative AI
text:
Exercise 5.8.1
Before reaching for AI, try writing this yourself — the first practical guideline from the video.
import pandas as pd

employees = pd.DataFrame({
    "name":       ["Anna", "Bjorn", "Cecilia", "David", "Elin", "Fredrik"],
    "department": ["Sales", "Sales", "IT", "IT", "Sales", "IT"],
    "salary":     [45000, 52000, 61000, 58000, 47000, 63000]
})

Without using AI, write code to compute the median salary for each department.
Now describe the same task in a prompt to an AI assistant of your choice, and compare the code it suggests to your own. Does it produce the same result? Is the approach essentially the same, or does it differ?



Solution


import pandas as pd

employees = pd.DataFrame({
    "name":       ["Anna", "Bjorn", "Cecilia", "David", "Elin", "Fredrik"],
    "department": ["Sales", "Sales", "IT", "IT", "Sales", "IT"],
    "salary":     [45000, 52000, 61000, 58000, 47000, 63000]
})

median_by_dept = employees.groupby("department")["salary"].median()
print(median_by_dept)

department
IT       61000.0
Sales    47000.0
Name: salary, dtype: float64


IT has a median salary of 61,000, and Sales has a median salary of 47,000. For a task this simple, an AI assistant will almost always suggest essentially the same groupby().median() approach — there is usually only one natural way to do something this standard. The value of trying it yourself first is not that AI would get it wrong, but that you build and retain the skill yourself, and you have an immediate way to check the AI’s suggestion against something you already know to be correct.




Exercise 5.8.2
AI tools are often useful for explaining an error message you do not understand. Suppose you wrote the following code and got an error:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({"name": "Alice", "age": 30})
ValueError: If using all scalar values, you must pass an index

Paste this code and error message into an AI assistant and ask it to explain what went wrong and how to fix it.
Write the explanation in your own words, and fix the code so it runs correctly.



Solution


import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({"name": ["Alice"], "age": [30]})
print(df)

    name  age
0  Alice   30


The error occurs because {"name": "Alice", "age": 30} gives pandas two plain scalar values (a single string and a single number) with nothing to tell it how many rows to create. A DataFrame needs to know its row count from somewhere — normally from the length of the values you provide. Wrapping each value in a list, {"name": ["Alice"], "age": [30]}, tells pandas there is exactly one row, which resolves the error. Check whatever explanation your AI assistant gave against this: a good explanation should mention that dictionary values need to be list-like (or that you must supply an explicit index=), not just say “add brackets” without explaining why.




Exercise 5.8.3
Try this yourself for a few minutes first. If you get stuck, ask an AI assistant.
Task: you have a bar chart, and you want to display the exact value above each bar, rather than making the reader estimate it from the y-axis.
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))
bars = ax.bar(["A", "B", "C"], [10, 25, 15])

Look for a way to add the number above each bar. If you cannot find it within a few minutes, ask an AI assistant something like: “How do I display the value of each bar above it in a matplotlib bar chart?”
Apply the suggested approach to the chart above.



Solution


import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))
bars = ax.bar(["A", "B", "C"], [10, 25, 15])

ax.bar_label(bars)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()








ax.bar_label() takes the container of bars returned by ax.bar() and automatically places each bar’s value directly above it. This is a genuinely useful matplotlib function that was not covered anywhere in this course — a realistic example of the kind of small, well-defined task that AI assistants are typically very good at answering quickly and correctly.




Exercise 5.8.4
Ask an AI assistant to write a function for you, then verify it against a result you can check by hand.
Prompt idea: “Write a Python function that computes the compound annual growth rate (CAGR) between a start value, an end value, and a number of years.”

Ask an AI assistant for this function, and copy the suggested code.
Test it using a start value of 100, an end value of 200, and 10 years. The correct answer is approximately 7.18%. Does the AI’s function produce this result?
Why is having a value you can check by hand — rather than just trusting that the code runs without an error — an important part of using AI-suggested code responsibly?



Solution


def cagr(start_value, end_value, years):
    return (end_value / start_value) ** (1 / years) - 1

result = cagr(100, 200, 10) * 100
print(f"CAGR: {result:.2f}%")

CAGR: 7.18%



This produces 7.18%, matching the expected value.
Code that runs without error is not the same as code that is correct. (end_value / start_value) ** (1 / years) - 1 is a specific formula — if an AI assistant made a subtle mistake, such as swapping the numerator and denominator, or using years - 1 instead of years, the function would still run without any error and return a plausible-looking number. Only checking the result against a value you can compute or verify independently would reveal a mistake like that — this is exactly the “plausible-but-wrong logic” failure mode described in the video, and it is why “always run the code and check the result” applies even when a task involves a formula you could look up and verify.





Exercise 5.8.5
Suppose an AI assistant suggested the following function to compute a rolling average over a list of numbers:
def rolling_avg(x, window=3):
    n = len(x)
    result = [None] * n
    for i in range(window, n):
        result[i] = sum(x[i-window:i]) / window
    return result

Test this function using x = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] and window=3. A rolling average with a window of 3 should have its first valid value at position 2 (using 0-indexing), computed as the average of the first three numbers: (1 + 2 + 3) / 3 = 2. Does the function’s output match this?
If it does not match, identify exactly which line causes the problem, and explain why.
Fix the function so that rolling_avg([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], window=3) returns [None, None, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0].



Solution


def rolling_avg_buggy(x, window=3):
    n = len(x)
    result = [None] * n
    for i in range(window, n):
        result[i] = sum(x[i-window:i]) / window
    return result

print(rolling_avg_buggy([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], window=3))

[None, None, None, 2.0, 3.0]



def rolling_avg(x, window=3):
    n = len(x)
    result = [None] * n
    for i in range(window - 1, n):
        result[i] = sum(x[i-window+1:i+1]) / window
    return result

print(rolling_avg([1, 2, 3, 4, 5], window=3))

[None, None, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0]



The buggy version returns [None, None, None, 2.0, 3.0] — every value is shifted one position later than it should be.
The loop starts at range(window, n), meaning the first computed value lands at position 3 rather than position 2. The slice x[i-window:i] for i=3 gives x[0:3], the average of the first three values — but it is stored at position 3 instead of position 2, where the third value actually belongs. The loop’s starting point and the slice indices are off by one relative to each other.
Starting the loop at window - 1 and using the slice x[i-window+1:i+1] aligns each computed average with the correct position — the average of the three values ending at position i is stored at position i itself. This kind of off-by-one error is exactly the sort of “plausible-but-wrong logic” mistake the video warns about: the function runs without any error and produces numbers that look entirely reasonable, and the only way to catch the problem is to test it against a small example where you already know what the correct answer should be.

title: Topic 5 — Further Exercises
text:
🟢 Warm-up
These exercises give you an opportunity to consolidate everything covered in Topic 5 before moving on. Many of these combine skills from several videos into a single task, and some may introduce new tools that were not shown in the videos — for these, you are expected to look up the documentation yourself, the same way you would when working independently.
The exercises are organised into three levels:

title: Topic 5 — Further Exercises
text:
Exercise 5.9.1
Fit a simple regression on the healthexp dataset.
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

healthexp = sns.load_dataset("healthexp")

Fit a regression of Life_Expectancy on the log of Spending_USD and print the .summary().
What is the coefficient on the log of Spending_USD? Since the explanatory variable is in logs, the interpretation is: a 1% increase in health spending is associated with an increase in life expectancy of coefficient/100 years. How many years of life expectancy is a 10% increase in spending associated with?
What is the R-squared, and what does it tell you?



Solution


import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

healthexp = sns.load_dataset("healthexp")
healthexp["log_spending"] = np.log(healthexp["Spending_USD"])

model = smf.ols("Life_Expectancy ~ log_spending", data=healthexp).fit()
